quality.line: Prefer products stating **beta-glucan %** (not just 'polysaccharides', which can hide added grain starch) and with **third-party heavy-metal testing** — mushrooms bioaccumulate metals from substrate.

nonResponders.line: The most common reason for 'nothing happened' is a **low-active mycelium-on-grain product** or quitting before ~8 weeks. NGF support is subtle and cumulative, not a same-day stimulant effect.

synergy.why: Both support neuronal membrane health and neurotrophic signalling; a reasonable pairing for cognitive goals, though direct combination trials are limited.
Exercise independently raises BDNF; stacking a neurotrophic-supporting lifestyle input plausibly complements Lion's Mane, though not directly tested together.

form.buy: Extract **standardized to a stated bacoside percentage** — bacosides are the actives responsible for the cognitive effect; clinical trials used material standardized to ~50-55% bacosides, so match your dose to bacoside content rather than raw milligrams.
form.avoid: Unstandardized **whole-herb powder of unknown bacoside content** — potency swings wildly and dosing becomes guesswork.
form.withFood: Take **with a meal containing some fat** — reduces the nausea/cramping and supports absorption of the saponin bacosides.
form.bioavailability: Bacosides are **amphipathic saponins with lipophilic character**; a fatty meal is traditionally used (historically ghee) and plausibly aids uptake, though human pharmacokinetic data are limited.

dosing.cap: ~300-450 mg/day standardized extract in trials; more is not better and worsens GI effects
dosing.note: Human trials mostly used **300 mg/day of extract standardized to ~50-55% bacosides**. Whole-herb powder equivalents run far higher and are hard to dose reliably.

timing.line: **Daily consistency beats time-of-day** — effects build over months, not hours. If it makes you drowsy or foggy, shift the dose to evening; if it disrupts sleep, keep it to the morning.

cycling.line: **Don't cycle it short.** The memory benefit only emerges after ~8-12 weeks of continuous daily use, so on/off cycling defeats the mechanism. There is no established need to cycle off.

nonResponders.line: The most common 'non-response' is **quitting too early** — a single dose or a few weeks does little, since the effect is on memory consolidation over ~12 weeks, not acute alertness. Give it a full 12-week trial before judging.

cost.math: A standardized-extract bottle (roughly 3-4 months at 300 mg/day) commonly runs ~SGD $20-30, i.e. ~SGD $5-10/month.
cost.note: One of the cheaper nootropics; cost is rarely the limiting factor over the long trial it requires.

quality.line: As an Ayurvedic botanical, prefer **third-party-tested extract screened for heavy metals** (lead, arsenic, mercury) with verified bacoside content — heavy-metal contamination is a documented concern across Ayurvedic herbal products.

needs.role: Taking with a fat-containing meal is traditional for these saponins and mainly helps reduce GI upset; any absorption benefit in humans is not well quantified.

biomarkers.why: Bacopa raised T4 (~40%) in rodent studies with T3 largely unchanged; unproven in humans, so periodic checks are precautionary for long-term users or anyone on thyroid medication.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and periodically if using long-term or on thyroid meds

contra.advice: Nausea, cramping, loose stools and increased stool frequency are the most common side effects. Take with food, start at a lower dose, and titrate up.
May increase T4 (animal data); use cautiously and monitor thyroid labs if you have thyroid disease or take thyroid hormone. Discuss with your doctor.
Bacopa can be mildly sedating; watch for additive drowsiness with alcohol, benzodiazepines or other sedatives.
Bacopa has cholinergic activity and may increase intestinal and bronchial secretions or slow heart rate; use caution with relevant cardiac, GI-ulcer, asthma/COPD or neurological medication.
Insufficient safety data; best avoided.

form.buy: **Pharmaceutical/USP-grade** low-dose oral solution or troche, third-party tested for heavy-metal contamination.
form.avoid: **Industrial, aquarium, or 'chemical-grade'** methylene blue — these can carry arsenic, lead, chromium and mercury and are not made for ingestion.
form.withFood: Take **with water**; can cause mild nausea or GI upset, so a small amount of food helps tolerability. It transiently stains the mouth blue.
form.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability is **~72%** (human PK study), so oral low-dose exposure is meaningful and reasonably predictable.

biomarkers.why: Methylene blue can trigger **acute hemolytic anemia** in G6PD deficiency, which is common in some Southeast Asian and Mediterranean backgrounds — screen before ever using it.
biomarkers.range: Normal enzyme activity per lab reference (deficiency flagged as low)

dosing.note: **Low dose only — the dose response is biphasic (hormetic):** the metabolic and cognitive benefits seen in research occur at low exposure and reverse into a pro-oxidant effect as the dose climbs. Self-directed nootropic use sits well under 1 mg/kg; hobbyist doses are typically in the single-to-low-double-digit milligram range, not the higher milligram-per-kg doses used clinically for methemoglobinemia.
dosing.cap: At higher exposure (roughly >2 mg/kg) the antioxidant effect flips **pro-oxidant** — more is not better. Note the serotonin-syndrome risk is separate and is **not** a high-dose-only phenomenon: clinically significant MAO inhibition occurs even below 1 mg/kg, so any combination with serotonergic drugs is dangerous at any dose.

timing.line: Take in the **morning** — it is mildly activating and its MAO-inhibiting action plus a ~5-6 h half-life can disrupt sleep if taken late.

contra.flag: **Serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, triptans, some opioids)**
contra.advice: Methylene blue is a potent MAO inhibitor and can precipitate **serotonin syndrome** — a genuine medical emergency — even at low doses. Do not combine; this is the single most important caution.
Risk of **hemolytic anemia**. Confirm G6PD status before use and avoid if deficient.
Avoid — methylene blue is a documented **teratogen** (fetal harm, neonatal complications) with inadequate safety data.
Harmless and expected — it stains urine, saliva and sometimes the whites of the eyes. Not a sign of toxicity.
Can cause a transient, falsely low SpO2 reading — mention recent use to any clinician measuring your oxygen saturation.

quality.line: Insist on **USP/pharmaceutical grade** with a certificate of analysis screening for heavy metals; reject industrial or aquarium-grade product regardless of price.

synergy.why: Both act on **cytochrome c oxidase** to support mitochondrial electron transport, and the combination is being explored for additive metabolic effect — mechanistically plausible but still emerging.

form.buy: Plain **free-form L-tyrosine** (powder or caps), single-ingredient, third-party tested.
form.avoid: **N-acetyl-L-tyrosine (NALT)** marketed as 'more bioavailable' — it deacetylates poorly, so a large fraction is excreted unchanged and it raises plasma tyrosine far less than plain L-tyrosine.
form.withFood: Take on an **empty stomach**, away from protein meals.
form.bioavailability: Crosses the blood-brain barrier via the **LAT1 transporter, which it shares with other large neutral amino acids** (tryptophan, phenylalanine, branched-chains); a protein-rich meal floods that transporter and blunts brain uptake.

dosing.cap: Practical use ~500 mg-2 g per dose; keep total under ~5-6 g/day.
dosing.note: Acute-stress studies dose **100-150 mg/kg** (roughly 7-11 g for a 70 kg adult) — experimental levels that commonly cause GI upset. Most users get situational benefit at 500 mg-2 g.

timing.line: Take **30-60 min before the stressor** — cold exposure, a demanding cognitive block, or a sleep-deprived shift — on an empty stomach. It is a substrate top-up, not a daily baseline booster.

cycling.line: Use **acutely / as-needed, not daily**. Benefit concentrates in catecholamine-depleting states (stress, cold, sleep loss); when you are rested it does little, so daily dosing mostly wastes it.

foodFirst.line: A normal diet already supplies several grams of **tyrosine plus phenylalanine** daily, so you are rarely deficient.
foodFirst.note: The supplement's edge is not extra tyrosine per se — it is delivering it **alone on an empty stomach**, so it isn't outcompeted by other amino acids at the brain transporter the way a protein meal's tyrosine is.

cost.math: A ~100 g pure powder tub runs roughly S$15-30, i.e. ~100 doses of 1 g.
cost.note: One of the cheapest nootropic raw materials; powder is far more economical than capsules.

needs.role: Non-heme iron cofactor for **tyrosine hydroxylase**, the rate-limiting enzyme converting tyrosine to L-DOPA; low iron caps the whole pathway regardless of substrate.
Cofactor for **aromatic amino acid decarboxylase**, which converts L-DOPA to dopamine.
Cofactor for **dopamine beta-hydroxylase**, converting dopamine to noradrenaline.
Metal cofactor for **dopamine beta-hydroxylase** alongside vitamin C.
May support **tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4)** regeneration, the essential cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase; the folate-BH4 link is real but indirect.

contra.advice: Do not combine — boosting catecholamine substrate alongside an **MAOI risks a hypertensive reaction**. Applies to prescription MAOIs and, cautiously, high-dose supplements with MAOI activity.
Large neutral amino acids **compete with levodopa for gut and brain-barrier transport** and can blunt its effect; separate timing and clear it with the treating physician.
Tyrosine is a **precursor to thyroid hormone**; those with an overactive thyroid or on thyroid medication should be cautious and check with a doctor.
Tyrosine is the **precursor to melanin**; caution is sometimes advised, though the clinical relevance of oral dosing is unproven — discuss with an oncologist.

quality.line: Choose **single-ingredient, third-party-tested** L-tyrosine (USP/NSF-type verification); avoid proprietary 'focus' blends that hide the tyrosine dose among stimulants.

nonResponders.line: If you are **well-rested and unstressed, expect little to nothing** — tyrosine only helps when acute stress, cold, or sleep loss has drawn down catecholamines. No effect in a fresh state is the expected result, not a bad batch.

synergy.why: These are the **enzymatic cofactors** downstream of tyrosine; adequate stores let the substrate actually convert to dopamine and noradrenaline.

synergy.with: Acute stressors (cold, sleep deprivation, high multitasking load)
synergy.why: The catecholamine-depleting **contexts where tyrosine's benefit shows up** in human studies — the 'synergy' is with the demand, not another pill.

biomarkers.why: Relevant only if you have thyroid concerns, since tyrosine feeds **thyroid hormone synthesis** — a monitoring check, not a routine one.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if symptoms arise, for those with thyroid conditions

form.buy: Available only as a grey-market nootropic or research chemical — there is no Western pharmaceutical supply. Insist on a recent third-party **certificate of analysis (COA)** for identity and purity.
form.avoid: Unlabelled powders with no COA — dose accuracy and contamination are the main real-world risks here.
form.withFood: Take with a meal containing some **dietary fat** — bromantane is a lipophilic adamantane derivative and lipophilic compounds are generally absorbed better with fat.
form.bioavailability: **Highly lipophilic** adamantane derivative that distributes into tissue; Russian data describe both acute effects (a single dose upregulates dopamine-synthesis enzymes) and a cumulative build-up over repeated dosing.

dosing.cap: ~100 mg/day; Russian asthenia trials used 50-100 mg/day for ~28 days
dosing.note: Benefit in the trials accrued over weeks of daily dosing, so resist dose-stacking early. No validated per-kg dosing exists; start low.

timing.line: Take in the **morning** — it is mildly alerting and evening doses can disrupt sleep despite its calm profile.

cycling.line: Commonly run in **short blocks** (e.g. 2-4 weeks on, then a break) rather than continuously; long-term safety data in humans is thin.

needs.role: Dietary **precursor** for the dopamine whose synthesis it drives via tyrosine hydroxylase upregulation.
**Tyrosine hydroxylase cofactor** (a non-heme iron enzyme); low iron caps the rate-limiting step of dopamine synthesis.
Cofactor for **aromatic amino acid decarboxylase**, the enzyme converting L-DOPA to dopamine.

foodFirst.line: Persistent fatigue is far more often driven by **poor sleep, low iron, or thyroid issues** — rule those out with basics and labs before reaching for a grey-market drug.

biomarkers.why: Iron is the tyrosine hydroxylase cofactor; low stores blunt the dopamine-synthesis mechanism bromantane relies on.

contra.advice: **Banned by WADA** (stimulant, with masking-agent characteristics); metabolites are detectable for up to ~2 weeks and a positive test ends seasons. Anyone subject to anti-doping testing must avoid it entirely.
Dopaminergic agents can **destabilise mood or trigger episodes** — avoid without psychiatric oversight.
Potential **additive dopaminergic load** — do not combine without medical guidance.
No safety data — **avoid**.

quality.line: There is no Western pharmaceutical supply, so purity rests entirely on the vendor's **third-party COA** — treat batches without one as unknown substances.

nonResponders.line: Effects are **subtle and cumulative**, not stimulant-like; some people feel little or nothing even after two weeks and should not chase it with higher doses.

synergy.why: Supplies the amino-acid **precursor** for the increased dopamine synthesis bromantane promotes, which may matter more under stress or low protein intake.

cycling.line: **Do not use regularly.** Tolerance builds within days (reported in as little as ~5 days) and physical dependence within 1-2 weeks of daily dosing; keep any use to no more than once or twice per week with several drug-free days in between, and never daily.

contra.advice: **Never combine** — additive CNS and respiratory depression can cause blackout, coma, or death.
Withdrawal is **benzodiazepine-like** (rebound anxiety, insomnia, hallucinations, delirium, seizures) and can be dangerous; taper slowly under medical supervision, never stop abruptly.
**Sedation and impaired coordination** persist for hours past peak; do not drive after dosing.
**High dependence liability** — best avoided entirely if you are prone to addiction.

timing.line: **Slow onset** — oral effects take roughly 2-4 hours to come on and peak later still. Redosing before onset is a leading cause of accidental overshoot and overdose; take one dose and wait.

dosing.cap: Keep well under 1 g per occasion; higher doses sharply raise dependence and toxicity risk with little added benefit.
dosing.note: **Less is safer** — start at the low end, allow the full slow onset before judging the effect, and never redose to chase it.

form.buy: **High-purity phenibut verified by a certificate of analysis.** HCl is the common water-soluble salt; freebase (FAA) is fat-soluble.
form.avoid: Unverified bulk powder and any eyeballed or volumetric (scoop) dosing.
form.withFood: Fine with or without food; the fat-soluble freebase may absorb better with a little dietary fat.
form.bioavailability: Orally active.

quality.line: Sold as an **unregulated grey-market supplement** — mislabeled potency and contamination are common. Insist on a certificate of analysis and weigh every dose on a **milligram scale**; eyeballed dosing is a top cause of accidental overdose.

form.buy: **Immediate-release, low-dose (0.3-0.5 mg)** tablets for circadian timing and jet lag; extended-release is aimed at sleep maintenance, not phase shifting.
form.avoid: **High-dose (5-10 mg) products and gummies** - more is not better for timing and gummy melatonin content is especially inconsistent versus label.
form.withFood: Take on a **relatively empty stomach**; a heavy meal delays and blunts the peak.
form.bioavailability: Oral **bioavailability is low and variable (~15%)** due to extensive first-pass metabolism, so effect size differs person to person.

foodFirst.line: The real 'food-first' lever is **light, not a pill**: bright light in the morning and dim, screen-reduced light in the evening drive your own melatonin release.
foodFirst.note: Behavioural circadian hygiene outperforms a supplement for ordinary sleep timing; reserve melatonin for jet lag and genuine phase-delay problems.

dosing.cap: ~3 mg for timing purposes - above this you gain grogginess and vivid dreams, not more phase shift.
dosing.note: **0.5 mg shifts the clock as effectively as far higher doses.** Dose choice is about timing, not amount; start at the lowest dose that works.

timing.line: **Timing is the whole game.** To advance a late clock (delayed sleep phase), take a low dose ~3-5 h before your habitual sleep onset. For eastward jet lag, take 0.5 mg at destination bedtime for a few nights; taken at the wrong hour it shifts you the wrong way.

cycling.line: Best used **situationally** (travel, a bounded phase-shift block) rather than indefinitely as a nightly sedative; it does not cause physical dependence, but ongoing nightly use should prompt you to reassess the underlying cause.

contra.advice: Synthetic melatonin is a **Prescription-Only Medicine under HSA** - it is not a legal OTC supplement locally; obtain it through a doctor rather than assuming it is a casual buy.
Melatonin is **immune-modulating**; use only with medical guidance if you have an autoimmune condition or take immunosuppressive therapy.
May interact with **warfarin, other anticoagulants, and sedatives**, and evidence in epilepsy is mixed - clear it with a prescriber.
Safety data are limited; **avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding** and use in children only under specialist supervision.
Higher doses can cause **morning hangover and impaired alertness**; do not drive if you feel residual sedation.

quality.line: Independent testing (31 products, 16 brands) found actual melatonin content deviating from roughly **83% below to nearly 480% above** the label, with serotonin contamination in about a quarter of products - favour a **third-party-tested / USP-verified** product and treat the stated dose skeptically.

nonResponders.line: People whose problem is **staying asleep or waking at 3am** respond less than those with a delayed clock; if low-dose timed melatonin does nothing after a fair trial, the issue is likely not circadian and warrants a sleep assessment.

synergy.why: **Morning bright light plus evening dim light** works with melatonin on the same circadian axis and is the stronger phase-shifting tool of the two.
A **fixed wake time** anchors the clock so a small melatonin dose has a clear, reproducible target to shift toward.

form.buy: High-purity (**98%+**) apigenin powder or capsules, third-party tested for identity and heavy metals; typical single-ingredient dose is 50 mg.
form.avoid: Proprietary 'sleep blend' capsules that bury an undisclosed apigenin amount among many actives — you can't dose or troubleshoot it.
form.withFood: Take with a **small amount of dietary fat** since it's lipophilic and poorly water-soluble; this may modestly aid absorption, though the effect is unproven.
form.bioavailability: Apigenin has **poor oral bioavailability** and low water solubility, so effects are gentle and inconsistent between people.

foodFirst.line: **Chamomile tea** before bed is the food-first route — apigenin is its signature calming flavone, alongside other mild sedative compounds.
foodFirst.note: Chamomile extract (not isolated apigenin) is what has actually been trialled for sleep; a cup of tea delivers far less apigenin than a 50 mg capsule and is more ritual-and-warmth than a pharmacological dose.

cost.math: A 60-capsule bottle of 50 mg apigenin runs roughly S$20–40 in Singapore, i.e. under a dollar a night.
cost.note: Rough range; pricing varies widely by purity and importer.

dosing.cap: 50 mg
dosing.note: Common biohacker practice is **50 mg once nightly**, but there are no published human trials of isolated apigenin for sleep — treat any dose as experimental and start at the low end. No basis for per-kg dosing.

timing.line: Take **30–60 minutes before bed**, on the nights you actually want the wind-down effect.

cycling.line: No established tolerance or dependence, so nightly use is reasonable; still, keep it as-needed rather than automatic so you can tell if it's doing anything.

contra.flag: Combined with alcohol, benzodiazepines, or other sedative sleep aids
contra.advice: It binds the benzodiazepine site of GABA-A receptors in lab studies, so a **theoretical additive effect with other CNS depressants** can't be ruled out — don't stack sedatives without medical guidance.
Insufficient safety data and some preclinical hormonal/uterine activity — **avoid**.

contra.flag: On medications with a narrow therapeutic window
contra.advice: Apigenin can **inhibit CYP3A4 and CYP2C9** in vitro, which could theoretically alter drug metabolism; check with a pharmacist if you take anything critical.

quality.line: Look for **98%+ purity** with a third-party certificate of analysis; cheap flavonoid powders are frequently underdosed or contaminated.

nonResponders.line: The effect is a **subtle nudge, not a sedative** — plenty of people feel nothing, which is normal and not a sign to keep raising the dose.

synergy.why: Common calming-sleep pairing; both are thought to support relaxation, though the combination is untested.
Promotes relaxed, non-sedating calm that plausibly complements apigenin's gentle wind-down.
Glycine (~3 g) has human data for faster sleep onset and lowers core body temperature — a mechanistically distinct partner, though not tested alongside apigenin.

form.buy: A **standardized whole-stigma extract**, characterized for its **crocin and safranal** content (most positive mood trials used ~30 mg/day of an extract standardized to these actives). This gives you a known dose rather than guessing from loose spice.
form.avoid: Cheap loose **saffron powder** and unstandardized 'saffron' capsules — saffron is one of the most **adulterated** botanicals on earth (dyed corn silk, safflower, marigold), so unverified material may contain little or no real Crocus sativus.
form.withFood: Take with or without food; a little food helps if you get mild nausea. Consistency matters more than timing.
form.bioavailability: Ingested **crocins are poorly absorbed intact** and are hydrolyzed in the gut to **crocetin**, which is the well-absorbed, blood-brain-barrier-crossing form — so total crocin/safranal content is a more useful spec than any single marker.

dosing.cap: ~30 mg/day standardized extract
dosing.note: The evidence-backed antidepressant/anxiolytic dose is **~28-30 mg/day of standardized extract** (often split as 15 mg twice daily). More is not better — higher doses add no benefit, and gram-level intakes of raw saffron (~5 g) become toxic. Per-kg dosing is not used.

timing.line: Take **daily and consistently**, splitting into morning and evening (15 mg + 15 mg) if you want steadier levels. Time of day is not critical; it is not a fast-acting mood 'hit'.

nonResponders.line: Like an SSRI, effect **builds over 4-6 weeks**, not in a day — judge it on a 6-8 week trial, not a single dose. If loose spice did nothing, the likely culprit is **adulteration or under-dosing**; a characterized standardized extract at 30 mg is the fair test.

cycling.line: No cycling needed — trials dose it **continuously** like a maintenance antidepressant, and there is no established tolerance or withdrawal. Stop if you see no benefit by ~8 weeks.

cost.math: 30 mg standardized extract; capsules typically S$0.60-1.20 per daily dose depending on standardization and testing.
cost.note: Real standardized saffron extract is genuinely pricey — suspiciously cheap 'saffron' is a red flag for adulteration.

quality.line: Adulteration is the #1 problem: prefer a **standardized extract with a stated crocin/safranal spec** and **third-party identity/purity testing**. Certification standards (e.g. USP-style verification) or a published HPLC fingerprint are reassuring; a generic 'saffron 8800:1' claim with no actives listed is not.

contra.advice: **Avoid supplemental doses in pregnancy** — saffron has uterine-stimulant/abortifacient activity at higher intakes. Culinary amounts in food are a separate matter, but concentrated extracts should be avoided.

contra.flag: On an SSRI/SNRI or other serotonergic drug
contra.advice: Saffron gently raises **serotonergic** tone; it has been studied as an *adjunct* to antidepressants, but combining is a medical decision — **clear it with your prescriber** rather than stacking on your own.
As with any antidepressant-like agent, there is a theoretical risk of a **manic/hypomanic switch** — do not self-treat mood without psychiatric oversight.
Saffron has mild **antiplatelet** activity; use caution alongside blood thinners and pause before surgery.

synergy.why: Trials have used saffron **added to standard antidepressants** with additional symptom improvement — but only under medical supervision given overlapping serotonergic action.
A **saffron + curcumin** combination has been studied for major depressive symptoms with additive benefit in small trials.

form.buy: **5-HTP** (extracted from *Griffonia simplicifolia* seed) or free-form **L-tryptophan**; choose a high-purity, third-party-tested product (USP- or NSF-verified).
form.avoid: Unverified bulk powder. L-tryptophan carries a historical contamination risk - the 1989 **EMS outbreak** was traced to a manufacturing contaminant (**'Peak E' / EBT**) in one maker's L-tryptophan, and a related impurity (**'Peak X'**) was later found in some 5-HTP - so verified purity matters more here than for most supplements.
form.withFood: **5-HTP** is absorbed without competing for the amino-acid transporter, so meal timing matters little. **L-tryptophan** competes with other large neutral amino acids, so take it away from high-protein meals; a small dose of carbohydrate improves its brain uptake.
form.bioavailability: Oral 5-HTP is well absorbed (mean ~70%, individual range roughly 45-85%) and bypasses the rate-limiting tryptophan-hydroxylase step, making it a more direct serotonin precursor than L-tryptophan.

needs.role: Cofactor for aromatic L-amino-acid decarboxylase, the enzyme that converts 5-HTP to serotonin; low B6 blunts the conversion.

foodFirst.line: Whole-food tryptophan sources (**turkey, eggs, dairy, pumpkin/sesame seeds, soy**) supply the raw material, but dietary tryptophan converts poorly to brain serotonin because it competes with other amino acids for transport.
foodFirst.note: Pairing tryptophan-rich food with some carbohydrate raises brain tryptophan uptake via insulin, which lowers competing amino acids in blood.

cost.math: A bottle of 60-120 capsules (100 mg) typically runs ~S$15-30, giving a low-teens-of-cents per-dose cost.
cost.note: Rough Singapore retail range; third-party-tested products sit at the higher end.

dosing.cap: 5-HTP is commonly used at 50-100 mg, up to ~300 mg/day in divided doses; L-tryptophan at 500 mg-2 g. Start at the low end.
dosing.note: No per-kg dosing standard exists. Titrate slowly; nausea is dose-related and the main limiter.

timing.line: For sleep, 100-200 mg 5-HTP taken 30-45 min before bed; for daytime mood, split into smaller divided doses. Take L-tryptophan on a relatively empty stomach, away from protein.

cycling.line: Some users cycle (e.g. weeks on / off) on the theory that continuous dosing may downregulate serotonin receptors, but evidence for a benefit from cycling is thin. Treat as optional, not established.

contra.flag: On any serotonergic drug (SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, triptans, tramadol, dextromethorphan, St John's Wort)
contra.advice: Do not combine - additive serotonin can cause **serotonin syndrome**, a medical emergency. This is the single most important restriction; clear it with a prescriber first.
Avoid - safety is not established.

contra.flag: Carcinoid syndrome or other serotonin-secreting tumors
contra.advice: Avoid - adds to an already elevated serotonin load.

contra.flag: History of eosinophilia-myalgia syndrome (EMS) or scleroderma-like illness
contra.advice: Avoid, or use only verified-purity material given the historical contamination link.

quality.line: The 1989 L-tryptophan **EMS epidemic** was tied to a manufacturing contaminant, not the molecule itself. Buy third-party-tested product (USP/NSF) rather than cheap bulk powder.

nonResponders.line: Without adequate B6, or with heavy peripheral decarboxylation, little reaches the brain and mood/sleep effects are muted. Others simply can't tolerate an effective dose because of nausea.

synergy.why: Cofactor for the decarboxylase that turns 5-HTP into serotonin - the classic 'take with B6' pairing.
Insulin lowers competing large neutral amino acids, increasing tryptophan transport into the brain.
Commonly stacked for sleep onset; complementary mechanisms, though evidence for the combination specifically is limited.

form.buy: **Glucosamine sulfate** (ideally crystalline glucosamine sulfate stabilised with sodium/potassium chloride) paired with **chondroitin sulfate** — the forms used in most positive trials.
form.avoid: **Glucosamine HCl** has weaker, largely negative trial evidence versus the sulfate form; low-dose 'joint' blends often under-deliver chondroitin.
form.withFood: Take **with meals** to blunt the mild GI upset (nausea, bloating) some people get.
form.bioavailability: Glucosamine is **well absorbed from the gut (~90%)** but heavy first-pass liver metabolism drops systemic bioavailability to roughly **~25%** (higher for crystalline glucosamine sulfate); chondroitin is a large molecule and **poorly absorbed (~10-15%)**, so labelled dose must be generous.

dosing.cap: No added benefit shown above ~1500 mg glucosamine + ~1200 mg chondroitin.
dosing.note: Standard trial doses: **1500 mg glucosamine sulfate + 800-1200 mg chondroitin sulfate daily**, as one dose or split. Per-kg dosing is not used.

timing.line: **Consistency beats timing** — take it every day; splitting into 2-3 doses with meals is fine and may ease GI upset. Effect builds over weeks, not hours.

cycling.line: **Run a defined 8-12 week trial**, then judge. It is a structural agent, not an acute painkiller, so a few skipped days won't 'reset' anything — and there's no benefit to intentional cycling.

nonResponders.line: A large share of people get **no meaningful benefit** (the GAIT trial found the combination no better than placebo overall). If pain and function haven't improved after **12 weeks, stop** — continuing rarely helps.

foodFirst.line: There is **no dietary source at therapeutic doses** — bone broth and cartilage contain only trace glucosamine/chondroitin, far below trial amounts.
foodFirst.note: For general joint health, resistance training, weight management and adequate protein have far stronger evidence than any supplement.

contra.advice: Glucosamine (especially with chondroitin) has multiple case reports and pharmacovigilance signals of **raised INR and bleeding risk** — avoid or monitor INR closely with your doctor.
Most glucosamine is made from shellfish shells. True reactions are rare (the allergen is in the flesh, not the chitin), but **corn-fermentation-derived (vegetarian) glucosamine** avoids the concern.
Insufficient safety data — **avoid** unless a clinician advises otherwise.
Early worry about worsened glucose control has **not held up** in controlled studies; still, monitor if diabetic.

biomarkers.why: Screens for the **bleeding-risk interaction** — the one lab genuinely worth tracking with this supplement.

cost.math: A month of combined glucosamine+chondroitin runs roughly SGD 15-35 depending on dose and purity.
cost.note: Cheapest way to test responsiveness is a single 3-month supply, then stop if no benefit.

quality.line: Independent testing repeatedly finds **chondroitin under-labelled** (products containing far less than stated). Prefer **third-party-tested (USP/NSF) products** with verified sulfate content.

synergy.why: Reducing joint load and strengthening surrounding muscle has **stronger, better-evidenced** effects on knee OA pain than the supplement itself.
Sometimes stacked for osteoarthritis for anti-inflammatory support, but **combined-benefit evidence is weak** — treat as experimental.

form.buy: **Standardized extract** guaranteeing boswellic acids (typically 65%+), and ideally a stated **AKBA** (acetyl-11-keto-beta-boswellic acid) percentage — AKBA is the most potent 5-LOX inhibitor among the boswellic acids. Enhanced-absorption forms (lecithin/phospholipid-complexed) raise plasma levels.
form.avoid: **Crude resin or unstandardized "frankincense"** with no boswellic-acid spec, and products that inflate cheaper beta-boswellic acid while hiding low AKBA.
form.withFood: Take **with a fat-containing meal** — boswellic acids are lipophilic and absorption is meaningfully higher with food.
form.bioavailability: Boswellic acids are **poorly absorbed**, and AKBA in particular has low oral bioavailability; taking with fat and choosing a phospholipid-delivery form are the main practical levers.

dosing.cap: Commonly up to ~1200 mg standardized extract/day in divided doses in trials; no established benefit above this.
dosing.note: Typical: **300-500 mg standardized extract, 2-3x daily**. AKBA-enriched extracts (e.g. ~30% AKBA) dose lower by weight — trials used ~100-250 mg of such extract per day, delivering only roughly **30-75 mg AKBA/day**. Per-kg dosing is not standard for boswellia.

timing.line: **Split into 2-3 doses with meals** across the day to maintain levels; joint-pain benefit builds over **days to weeks** (some trials show improvement within ~1 week), not as a single acute dose.

cycling.line: No cycling required — it is used **continuously** in osteoarthritis trials lasting weeks to months. Reassess benefit at ~8-12 weeks and stop if no change.

contra.advice: Avoid — traditionally regarded as an emmenagogue and lacking safety data in pregnancy.
As a 5-LOX/leukotriene inhibitor there is a theoretical additive bleeding effect; discuss with a clinician before combining.

contra.flag: On drugs with narrow therapeutic windows
contra.advice: Boswellia can modulate CYP (e.g. CYP3A4/2D6) enzymes in vitro; if you take critical medications (immunosuppressants, some statins, anticonvulsants), check for interactions.
The most common real-world effects are mild — nausea, reflux, loose stools; taking with food usually resolves them.

quality.line: Choose **third-party-tested, standardized** extract that specifies both total boswellic acids and AKBA; unstandardized resin varies wildly and cheaper Boswellia species or beta-boswellic-acid padding are common adulteration routes.

nonResponders.line: Products low in **AKBA** (the active 5-LOX inhibitor) are a frequent reason for no effect — if a low-standardization product did nothing, an AKBA-enriched or enhanced-absorption form is the meaningful next test before concluding you are a non-responder.

synergy.why: The classic joint pairing — curcumin acts mainly on NF-kB/COX-linked signaling while boswellia hits the 5-LOX pathway; small trials of the combination show osteoarthritis benefit and the mechanisms are complementary rather than redundant.

Fish oil shifts eicosanoid production toward less-inflammatory leukotrienes (LTB5 over LTB4), plausibly complementing 5-LOX inhibition; the fat also aids boswellic-acid absorption when taken together.

biomarkers.why: General systemic inflammation marker; useful to track a personal trend if you are using boswellia for an inflammatory condition, though it will not move for isolated mechanical joint pain.
biomarkers.range: <1 mg/L lower cardiovascular/inflammatory risk; 1-3 mg/L average; >3 mg/L elevated

biomarkers.why: Non-specific inflammation marker sometimes tracked in inflammatory arthritis; only meaningful alongside a diagnosed inflammatory condition, not for general use.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and follow-up if managing an inflammatory joint condition

needs.role: Lipophilic boswellic acids absorb substantially better when co-ingested with fat, so pairing doses with a meal is the simplest way to improve exposure.

cost.math: A month of standardized extract runs roughly SGD 20-40; enhanced-absorption/AKBA-enriched forms sit at the higher end.
cost.note: Rough Singapore retail ranges; verify against current standardization and serving size.

form.buy: Standardized **bergamot polyphenolic fraction (BPF)** — look for a stated polyphenol/flavonoid content (commonly standardized to ~38%), low furanocoumarin content, and independent third-party testing.
form.avoid: **Bergamot essential oil** — it is rich in phototoxic furanocoumarins (bergapten) and is not the cholesterol-active polyphenol extract.
form.withFood: Take with a meal to aid absorption of the flavonoids and reduce any GI upset.
form.bioavailability: Citrus flavonoids have **modest oral bioavailability**; standardized whole-fraction extracts are what nearly all human lipid trials used.

biomarkers.why: Primary and best-supported target — bergamot reliably lowers **LDL-C** in trials, via HMG-CoA reductase inhibition (a statin-like moiety in brutieridin/melitidin) among several proposed mechanisms.
Bergamot **often lowers fasting triglycerides**, though the effect is variable and largest in those with high baseline TG.
Tracks the overall lipid effect alongside LDL and is consistently reduced in trials.
Emerging data suggest **modest glycemic improvement**; worth watching if you have metabolic risk.

foodFirst.line: Diet moves LDL more than any extract: **soluble fiber** (oats, beans, psyllium), swapping saturated for unsaturated fat, and ~2g/day plant sterols are the proven first layer.
foodFirst.note: Bergamot is a top-up for mild dyslipidemia, not a substitute for those basics.

cost.math: A month of standardized BPF typically runs ~$25-45 SGD.
cost.note: Price varies widely by polyphenol concentration — cheaper products are often under-standardized.

dosing.cap: ~1500 mg/day
dosing.note: Human trials used roughly **500-1000 mg standardized BPF daily**, sometimes split with meals; 1000 mg outperformed 500 mg on LDL, but more is not clearly better.

timing.line: Endogenous cholesterol synthesis peaks overnight, so an **evening dose with dinner** is a reasonable rationale (mirroring statin timing), though not proven superior for bergamot.

cycling.line: No cycling needed — it is used continuously. **Reassess your lipid panel at ~12 weeks** and decide whether the effect justifies staying on it.

contra.flag: On a statin or other lipid-lowering drug
contra.advice: Do not self-substitute or silently stack — discuss with your doctor and keep monitoring your lipid panel.
Insufficient safety data — avoid.
Residual bergamot furanocoumarins can inhibit CYP3A4 (grapefruit-like); prefer low-furanocoumarin standardized extracts and check for drug interactions.

quality.line: Prefer extracts with a **stated, standardized polyphenol content**, low furanocoumarin/bergapten, and independent testing (e.g. NSF or USP) — potency between products varies enormously.

nonResponders.line: LDL response is variable; if a lipid panel shows **no meaningful change after ~12 weeks** at an adequate dose, it is unlikely to work for you — stop rather than escalate.

synergy.why: Complementary mechanism — sterols block **cholesterol absorption** while bergamot mainly acts on synthesis/clearance.
Adds a separate LDL-lowering pathway via **bile-acid binding**.
Studied together for **combined lipid and glycemic** benefit via distinct mechanisms.

form.buy: Look for **enteric-coated capsules standardized in fibrinolytic units (FU)** — e.g. 2000 FU per dose — from a maker that third-party tests for enzyme activity and purity.
form.avoid: **mg-only products that don't state FU activity** (mg tells you nothing about how active the enzyme is), and combination products with added vitamin K if you take an anticoagulant.
form.withFood: Enteric coating survives stomach acid, so it can be taken **with or without food**; many take it on an empty stomach or at night for consistency.
form.bioavailability: It's a large protein, so oral absorption is debated, but **enteric coating protects it from gastric acid** and measurable BP effects have been shown in RCTs.

biomarkers.why: The most consistent measurable effect; a meta-analysis of RCTs shows a modest SBP/DBP reduction (~3-5 mmHg) over 8 weeks.
biomarkers.range: Target <130/80 mmHg; track trend, not single readings
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then weekly averages over an 8-week trial

biomarkers.why: A single small open-label trial reported lower fibrinogen and factors VII/VIII; a plausible read on fibrinolytic action but not confirmed in controlled trials.
Reflects fibrin turnover; useful context if tracking clot-related risk, though noisy and non-specific.

foodFirst.line: **Natto** — the fermented soybean it's extracted from — delivers the enzyme plus fibre, protein and vitamin K2 in whole-food form.
foodFirst.note: The catch: natto is naturally high in vitamin K2, which antagonises warfarin — a reason purified capsules exist for those needing the enzyme without the K.

cost.math: A month of standardised capsules typically runs ~SGD 20-40.
cost.note: Rough Singapore retail range; price tracks stated FU activity more than capsule count.

dosing.cap: Most trials used 2000-4000 FU/day; no established benefit to going higher
dosing.note: **2000 FU (~100 mg) once daily** is the common studied dose. Potency is measured in FU, not mg — dose by activity. Per-kg dosing is not used for this enzyme.

timing.line: Take it **consistently at the same time daily**; some prefer evening dosing given higher overnight clotting tendency, though this timing benefit is unproven.

contra.flag: **On anticoagulants or antiplatelets** (warfarin, DOACs, aspirin, clopidogrel)
contra.advice: Plausible additive bleeding risk — do not combine without a doctor's oversight and monitoring.
Stop at least 1-2 weeks before any procedure; avoid entirely with clotting disorders.
Not studied for safety — avoid.
It's derived from fermented soy; choose accordingly or avoid.

quality.line: Because activity varies by extraction, prefer products that **state FU per dose and third-party verify the enzyme activity**, not just the milligram weight.

nonResponders.line: The blood-pressure effect is **modest and not universal** — it is not a substitute for prescribed antihypertensives or for managing established cardiovascular disease.

form.buy: Concentrated **beetroot juice or 'shots' standardized to nitrate content** (target ~400 mg / 6.4 mmol nitrate per serving) — this is the only way to dose reliably.
form.avoid: Products that list only 'beetroot extract' with **no stated nitrate content** — nitrate varies widely between crops and processing methods, so an unlabelled powder tells you nothing about dose.
form.withFood: Fine with or without food; a small dose of vitamin C alongside can help preserve nitrite in the acidic stomach.
form.bioavailability: Activity depends on the **enterosalivary pathway**: swallowed nitrate is concentrated in saliva and reduced to nitrite by tongue bacteria — the oral step is the rate-limiter.

biomarkers.why: The main measurable effect; meta-analyses show a ~3-5 mmHg systolic drop with regular dietary nitrate. Track it, since this is what you can actually see change.
biomarkers.range: Optimal <120/80 mmHg; measure seated, rested, same time of day
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then after 1-2 weeks of consistent daily use

biomarkers.why: Harmless pigment (betalain) marker, not a health signal — noted only so you don't mistake it for blood.
biomarkers.when: Within a day of a beetroot dose

needs.role: Convert swallowed nitrate to nitrite — the obligatory first step to nitric oxide. Without them dietary nitrate does almost nothing.
Acidic environment drives conversion of nitrite to nitric oxide; strongly acid-suppressing conditions blunt the pathway.

antagonists.role: Wipes out the tongue bacteria that reduce nitrate, abolishing the blood-pressure and performance effect and even raising BP in some studies.

foodFirst.line: **Get nitrate from food first**: rocket/arugula, spinach, beetroot, celery and other leafy greens are the richest sources — a large salad can match a beet shot.
foodFirst.note: Whole-food nitrate comes bundled with potassium and polyphenols that independently support vascular function; the concentrate is just a convenient, dose-controlled version.

cost.perDose: ~S$2-4 per standardized shot; whole beetroot/greens are a fraction of that
cost.math: One concentrated shot delivering ~400 mg nitrate is roughly S$2-4; a bunch of raw beets or bag of rocket delivering similar nitrate is often under S$1 per serving.
cost.note: The premium is for convenience and a guaranteed nitrate dose, not superior biology.

dosing.cap: ~400-800 mg (6.4-13 mmol) per dose; more is not clearly better
dosing.note: Ergogenic and blood-pressure studies cluster around **~400 mg (6.4 mmol) nitrate** per serving. Per-kg dosing is not standard for nitrate — use the fixed-dose target instead.

timing.line: Plasma nitrite peaks **~2-3 hours after ingestion**, so take a performance dose 2-3 h before exercise; for blood pressure, timing matters less than taking it consistently every day.

cycling.line: No cycling needed — benefits hold with **daily continuous use**, and blood-pressure effects actually build over 1-2 weeks of consistent intake.

contra.flag: On **blood-pressure medication** or already low BP
contra.advice: Effect is additive and can cause lightheadedness; monitor BP and discuss with your doctor before adding a daily dose.
Beetroot is high in oxalate; stone-formers should favour lower-oxalate nitrate sources (e.g. rocket) or moderate beet intake.
The rinse cancels most of the benefit — skip it, or don't expect nitrate to work while you use it.

quality.line: Choose products that **state nitrate content per serving** and are third-party tested for heavy metals (root vegetables can concentrate soil contaminants); neutral certifications like NSF or Informed Sport are a reasonable filter.

nonResponders.line: **Non-responders** are common: mouthwash users, people lacking the right oral bacteria ('low reducers'), and those on strong acid-suppressing conditions may see little to no effect regardless of dose.

synergy.why: Nitrate lowers the oxygen cost of a given workload, complementing endurance work — the best-studied use case.
Antioxidant co-factors help preserve nitrite and nitric oxide, and are naturally present in the whole-food sources.
Raises nitric oxide through the separate arginine pathway, so it can stack additively with the nitrate-nitrite route.

form.buy: Products that print the full **strain designation** (genus + species + strain code, e.g. *Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG*) and guarantee **CFU through end of shelf-life**, not 'at time of manufacture'.
form.avoid: Labels showing only genus/species with no strain code, 'proprietary blends' hiding per-strain counts, or CFU stated only at manufacture.
form.withFood: Take with or just before a meal — buffering and slowed gastric emptying improve organism survival past stomach acid.
form.bioavailability: **Delayed-release / enteric capsules** and food-matrix delivery raise the fraction of live organisms reaching the colon.

foodFirst.line: **Fermented foods** — plain yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut, kimchi, miso, tempeh — deliver live cultures in a food matrix plus fiber, and suit 'general' gut goals well.
foodFirst.note: Heat-treated or pasteurized versions (shelf-stable sauerkraut, cooked miso) have few live organisms; choose refrigerated, unpasteurized products.

cost.math: A month of a multi-strain product runs roughly SGD 30-60; refrigerated fermented foods are often cheaper per serving.
cost.note: Higher CFU counts and more strains cost more but are not automatically better — match strain to goal, not price.

dosing.note: Most studied benefits sit in the **1-50 billion CFU/day** range and are strain-specific — the dose that worked in a trial for that exact strain is the guide, not a universal number. More CFU is not reliably better.
dosing.cap: No fixed cap; escalate only if a specific strain's evidence supports it.

timing.line: Take **daily and consistently** — effects depend on continued presence. If on antibiotics, separate the probiotic by at least 2 hours from each antibiotic dose.

cycling.line: Most strains are **transient** — they don't permanently colonize, so benefits fade within days to weeks of stopping. Continuous use is generally needed to sustain effects rather than cycling.

needs.role: Fermentable substrate that feeds beneficial bacteria; pairing the two is termed a **synbiotic**.

antagonists.role: Kill live probiotic organisms; space each dose at least 2 hours apart. Concurrent use during a course has moderate evidence for reducing antibiotic-associated diarrhea.

contra.flag: Immunocompromised, critically ill, or central venous catheter
contra.advice: Rare but documented cases of **bacteremia/fungemia** (including Saccharomyces boulardii fungemia via catheters); use only under medical supervision in these groups.
Some strains and prebiotic fibers can **worsen bloating**; introduce slowly and stop if symptoms increase.

quality.line: Favor products with **third-party verification** of strain identity and viable CFU; cold-chain handling (refrigerated shipping/storage) matters for many live-culture formulas.

nonResponders.line: Benefits are **strain-specific** — a strain mismatched to your goal often does nothing. If a well-chosen strain shows no effect after 4-8 weeks of consistent use, switching strains is more productive than raising the dose.

synergy.why: Provides fuel for the organisms; the combined synbiotic approach can outperform either alone.
A varied fiber intake supports a broader resident microbiome, complementing supplemented strains.

form.buy: Plain **L-glutamine free-form powder** (single-ingredient), third-party tested — it dissolves clear and near-tasteless in water. Look for USP or NSF verification.
form.avoid: Proprietary 'gut healing' or 'recovery' blends where glutamine is buried among undisclosed doses; capsules are fine but expensive per gram versus powder.
form.withFood: Take on an **empty stomach** in water so it isn't diluted by a large protein meal; between meals or before bed works well for gut-lining exposure.
form.bioavailability: Readily absorbed but **heavily extracted first-pass by the splanchnic bed (gut and liver)** — enterocytes are among the body's primary glutamine consumers, which is part of why oral glutamine suits a gut-targeted goal.

biomarkers.why: Objective marker of **gut-lining inflammation** — the clearest way to see whether glutamine is doing anything if you're using it for an irritated or inflamed gut rather than for vague symptoms.
biomarkers.range: <50 µg/g typically normal; 50-200 borderline; >200 suggests active inflammation (assay-dependent)
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then after 6-8 weeks of use

foodFirst.line: **Dietary protein already supplies ~3-6 g glutamine per day** (more on high-protein omnivore diets) — it is abundant in whey, meat, eggs, dairy, and cabbage/bone broth, so a well-fed athlete is rarely deficient.
foodFirst.note: Supplementation makes sense for a compromised, catabolic, or post-illness gut — not as a default add-on for someone eating enough protein. The body also makes roughly 50-80 g/day endogenously.

cost.math: 500 g plain powder runs ~$20-40 SGD = 100 doses of 5 g
cost.note: One of the cheapest supplements per gram; blends and capsules cost several times more for the same glutamine.

dosing.cap: Common self-directed range is 5 g once or twice daily; gut-repair protocols use up to ~15-20 g/day split into doses. There is little reason for a healthy person to exceed this.
dosing.note: Higher clinical doses (0.3-0.5 g/kg) exist only for critical illness and burns under supervision — not a consumer target. No reliable added benefit for healthy training adults above ~5-10 g/day.

timing.line: For gut-lining support, **split into 2 doses on an empty stomach** (e.g. morning and before bed) so enterocytes get more sustained exposure rather than one large bolus.

cycling.line: No dependence or receptor downregulation, so continuous use is fine — but most people run it as a **defined 4-8 week gut-repair course** and then reassess rather than taking it indefinitely.

contra.advice: Glutamine is metabolized to glutamate and **ammonia** — with significant liver disease it can worsen encephalopathy. Avoid unless a hepatologist directs it.
Some tumors are **glutamine-avid** and use it as fuel; the clinical relevance of supplements is unsettled, so discuss with your oncologist before using.
Amino-acid nitrogen load; get clearance from your doctor before supplementing above dietary levels.
Glutamine can convert to glutamate; if you get headaches or flushing from MSG, start with a low dose and monitor.

quality.line: Choose **third-party tested (USP/NSF)** powder — amino-acid supplements can carry heavy-metal or filler contamination, and 'L-glutamine' should be the only listed ingredient.

nonResponders.line: **Healthy, well-fed athletes typically feel nothing** — controlled trials show little benefit for muscle, immunity, or performance when glutamine stores are already full. The upside concentrates in damaged, inflamed, or catabolic guts.

synergy.why: Independently supports **gastric and intestinal mucosal repair**; commonly paired with glutamine in gut-lining protocols.
Glutamine fuels enterocytes while fermentable fiber feeds colonocytes via **butyrate** — complementary energy sources for different parts of the gut lining.
Both regulate **mucosal barrier integrity and gut immune tone**, supporting the same barrier that glutamine feeds.

form.buy: Plain **ascorbic acid** or buffered **sodium/calcium ascorbate** (gentler on an empty stomach); powder and tablets are pharmacologically equivalent.
form.avoid: No need to pay up for **liposomal or 'esterified' (calcium ascorbate)** formulations — the real-world absorption edge is marginal at everyday oral doses.
form.withFood: Take with food if plain ascorbic acid causes reflux; **buffered forms** cut the acidity.
form.bioavailability: Fractional absorption **saturates near ~200 mg per dose** (near-complete below this, falling to ~50% at 1000 mg) — splitting 2x 500 mg through the day beats one 1000 mg hit.

biomarkers.why: Confirms true deficiency vs adequacy; rarely needed unless malabsorption or a very restricted diet is suspected.
biomarkers.range: Deficiency <11 µmol/L; inadequate 11-23; adequate ~50-70; lab reference ranges often report ~23-85 µmol/L
biomarkers.when: Only if scurvy or deficiency is clinically suspected

depletes.role: Chronic megadoses (>1 g/day) may modestly impair copper status/ceruloplasmin.

foodFirst.line: One **red bell pepper (~95-150 mg)** or an orange (~70 mg) covers the 75-90 mg daily need — deficiency is rare on any diet containing fruit or veg.
foodFirst.note: In people already replete, supplementing above dietary intake has not shown consistent general-health benefit.

cost.math: ~$10-15 SGD for 100+ x 500 mg tablets
cost.note: One of the cheapest supplements available — no reason to overpay for fancy forms.

dosing.cap: Tolerable upper limit **2000 mg/day** — GI distress and rising urinary oxalate above this.
dosing.note: RDA 90 mg men / 75 mg women; +35 mg for smokers. Common supplemental doses are 250-1000 mg.

timing.line: Split across the day with food; because absorption is **saturable**, smaller frequent doses hold plasma levels higher than one large dose.

cycling.line: No cycling needed — water-soluble, and excess is renally excreted rather than stored.

contra.advice: Keep supplemental doses ≤1 g/day; high intake raises urinary oxalate.
Vitamin C boosts non-heme iron absorption — avoid high doses taken with meals.
Very high-dose (especially IV) vitamin C can trigger hemolysis.
High doses can skew glucometer and fecal occult-blood readings — pause before testing.

quality.line: Ascorbic acid is a simple, stable molecule — **third-party or USP-verified** is reassuring but purity problems are rare.

synergy.why: Vitamin C is a required cofactor for **collagen synthesis** (prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase); ~15 g collagen/gelatin + vitamin C taken ~60 min before loading was linked to improved collagen synthesis markers in small human trials (Shaw 2017).
Reduces Fe3+ to Fe2+, **markedly increasing absorption** — pair it with plant iron sources.
Can regenerate oxidized vitamin E in vitro; clinical relevance at dietary doses is modest.

form.buy: Prefer **active forms**: L-5-methylfolate (5-MTHF) over folic acid, **methyl- or adenosyl-cobalamin** over cyanocobalamin, and **P5P (pyridoxal-5-phosphate)** over plain pyridoxine — most relevant for folate with MTHFR variants, and generally with absorption issues.
form.avoid: Avoid chronic **high-dose pyridoxine HCl** (the adult UL is 100 mg/day) — sustained excess causes dose-dependent sensory neuropathy, with cases reported on chronic 75-300 mg/day.
form.withFood: Water-soluble; take **with food** to blunt the mild nausea a full B-complex can cause on an empty stomach.
form.bioavailability: Oral B12 absorption via intrinsic factor caps at ~1.5-2 mcg/dose, but ~1% is absorbed passively — so **high-dose oral (500-1000 mcg) works for most**; true pernicious anemia may still need IM or very-high-dose oral.

biomarkers.why: Functional readout of B6/B12/folate methylation status — rises when any of the three is low.
Specific **functional B12** marker — elevated in true deficiency even when serum B12 reads 'normal'.
First-line screen, but the low-normal band is unreliable — confirm borderline results with MMA/homocysteine.
The fraction actually deliverable to cells — a more sensitive early marker than total serum B12.
biomarkers.range: Low <35 pmol/L (35-40 commonly used as cutoff)

biomarkers.why: Reflects tissue folate stores over ~3 months rather than recent intake, unlike serum folate.

needs.role: FAD is the cofactor for the MTHFR enzyme; low B2 worsens methylation in people with MTHFR variants.
Required to release folate from its methyl form — without it folate gets stuck (methyl-folate trap).
Cofactor in several methylation-cycle and energy-metabolism reactions.

depletes.role: Rapid correction of a real B12 deficiency triggers red-cell production that can transiently drop serum potassium.

antagonists.role: Long-term use measurably reduces B12 absorption — periodic B12 checks warranted.
Suppress gastric acid needed to liberate food-bound B12, lowering absorption over time.
Impairs folate absorption and accelerates its excretion.
Can correct anemia while masking an underlying B12 deficiency, delaying diagnosis of neuro damage.

foodFirst.line: **B12 comes only from animal foods** (meat, fish, eggs, dairy); folate from leafy greens and legumes; B6 from poultry, fish, and starchy vegetables.
foodFirst.note: Vegans/vegetarians and adults >50 (reduced acid-mediated absorption) genuinely need supplemental B12 — plants are not a reliable source.

cost.perDose: A few cents SGD per day
cost.math: A quality B-complex runs ~$15-30 SGD for 60-90 servings
cost.note: One of the cheapest supplement categories — no reason to over-spend on megadose formulas.

dosing.cap: Chronic B6 <100 mg/day (UL, neuropathy); folic acid <1000 mcg/day to avoid masking B12 deficiency
dosing.note: No per-kg dosing. RDAs are small: B12 2.4 mcg, folate 400 mcg DFE, B6 1.3-1.7 mg. Therapeutic repletion is typically B12 500-1000 mcg and methylfolate 400-1000 mcg daily — more is not better.

timing.line: Take in the **morning** — B vitamins are mildly energizing, and late dosing disrupts sleep in some people.

cycling.line: Water-soluble, so **daily use is fine and no cycling is needed** — the one exception is high-dose B6, which should not be taken continuously long-term.

contra.advice: Check B12 status **before** starting high-dose folate — folate alone can resolve the anemia while B12-driven nerve damage progresses.

contra.flag: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy / heavy smokers
contra.advice: Avoid cyanocobalamin; use hydroxo- or methylcobalamin instead (cyanide-handling concern).
A minority report anxiety, irritability or insomnia on active forms ('overmethylation') — start low and titrate.
Use caution with cyanocobalamin; hydroxocobalamin is generally preferred.

quality.line: Choose **third-party-tested** products (e.g. NSF or USP certified) that clearly state the active forms (5-MTHF, methyl/adenosyl-B12, P5P) and avoid gratuitous megadose B6.

nonResponders.line: **Pernicious anemia / lack of intrinsic factor** absorbs oral B12 poorly and needs IM or very-high-dose oral; homozygous MTHFR carriers often respond better to methylfolate than folic acid.

synergy.why: Correcting folate without adequate B12 can worsen B12-related neuropathy — dose them as a pair, not folate alone.
Combined supplementation lowers homocysteine more than any single vitamin.
Improves homocysteine (and blood pressure) specifically in people with the MTHFR 677 TT genotype.

form.buy: **Ferrous bisglycinate** (chelated) is gentler on the gut at similar absorption; **ferrous sulfate/fumarate/gluconate** are cheap standards. Check the label states **elemental iron**, not salt weight.
form.avoid: Avoid slow-release or enteric-coated forms if correcting a real deficiency — they can release iron past the main absorption site (duodenum) and underperform.
form.withFood: Best absorbed on an empty stomach, but that worsens nausea; if needed take with a small non-dairy meal plus vitamin C, and keep coffee, tea, dairy and calcium supplements ~2h away.
form.bioavailability: **Heme iron** (meat) is ~15-35% absorbed and resists inhibitors; **non-heme iron** (supplements, plants) is ~2-20% and swings widely with body iron status, vitamin C and inhibitors.

biomarkers.why: Best single marker of iron stores — order it BEFORE supplementing to confirm deficiency and avoid loading iron you don't need.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then recheck ~8-12 weeks after starting

biomarkers.why: Distinguishes deficiency from overload; a high value is an early flag for iron loading.
Detects whether deficiency has progressed to anemia and tracks recovery.
Ferritin rises with inflammation, so a normal-looking ferritin can hide real deficiency — check CRP to interpret it.
biomarkers.range: <3 mg/L; higher means ferritin may be falsely elevated

needs.role: Reduces ferric to ferrous iron and forms a soluble complex, markedly boosting non-heme absorption.
Required (via ceruloplasmin) to load iron onto transferrin; deficiency causes an iron-refractory anemia.
May help mobilize iron from stores; deficiency can impair iron utilization, though the effect is less certain in well-nourished people.

depletes.role: High-dose iron taken at the same time can lower zinc absorption; separate them or take zinc away from iron.

antagonists.role: Inhibits both heme and non-heme absorption; don't co-dose with dairy or calcium supplements.
Sharply reduce non-heme absorption when taken with the dose.
Bind non-heme iron in the gut and lower absorption.

foodFirst.line: **Red meat, liver, poultry and fish** deliver well-absorbed heme iron; legumes and dark leafy greens give non-heme iron — pair those with a vitamin-C source (citrus, peppers).
foodFirst.note: Heme iron shrugs off most absorption inhibitors, and food sources are very unlikely to cause overload in people without hereditary hemochromatosis.

cost.math: A bottle of a basic ferrous salt is roughly SGD 10-20 for 1-3 months; chelated bisglycinate runs a little higher.
cost.note: Iron is one of the cheapest supplements — cost is never the limiting factor, testing and monitoring are.

dosing.cap: Do not exceed ~45 mg/day elemental (adult tolerable upper limit) without medical supervision
dosing.note: RDA is ~8 mg (men, postmenopausal women) and ~18 mg (premenopausal women). Deficiency is typically corrected with ~40-80 mg elemental/day; trial data suggest alternate-day dosing raises fractional absorption (less hepcidin blunting) than consecutive daily dosing. Doses always refer to elemental iron, not the salt.

timing.line: Take one dose in the morning on a relatively empty stomach with vitamin C; keep coffee, tea, dairy and calcium ~2h away. Alternate-day rather than twice-daily dosing may absorb better and is gentler on the gut.

cycling.line: This is corrective, not chronic — supplement only to fix a documented deficiency, then stop and retest ferritin. Ongoing use without monitoring risks silent iron loading.

contra.advice: Do not supplement — extra iron accumulates in liver, heart and pancreas and is toxic. Check TSAT/ferritin first.
Rarely need iron and are at higher overload risk; supplement only with lab-confirmed deficiency, not on symptoms alone.
Iron can accumulate to harmful levels even with anemia present — supplement only under specialist guidance.
Keep well out of reach — acute iron overdose is a leading cause of pediatric poisoning deaths.
Iron binds levothyroxine and certain antibiotics (reducing both); PPIs and low stomach acid cut iron absorption. Separate doses by several hours.

quality.line: Choose third-party-tested products (USP or NSF verified) and confirm the label lists **elemental iron** per dose rather than total salt weight, which is what you actually absorb.

nonResponders.line: If ferritin won't rise, look for ongoing blood loss (GI bleed, heavy menses), malabsorption (celiac, H. pylori, low stomach acid/PPI use), or inflammation driving hepcidin — an unaddressed cause, not a bigger dose, is usually the issue.

synergy.why: Taken in the same dose, it substantially increases non-heme iron absorption.
Needed to move iron onto transferrin for delivery; adequate copper prevents a functional bottleneck in iron use.
May support mobilization of stored iron and improve response to supplementation in deficient states.

form.buy: **Potassium iodide** gives a defined, measurable iodine dose; **selenomethionine** is the best-absorbed organic selenium form. Choose products with independent third-party testing (e.g. USP/NSF verified).
form.avoid: **High-dose kelp** and Lugol's-style iodine tinctures for casual use — iodine content is variable and easy to overshoot; **sodium selenite** is somewhat less well retained than selenomethionine.
form.withFood: Take **with food** to reduce GI upset; absorption is not strongly food-dependent.
form.bioavailability: **Selenomethionine** is efficiently absorbed and incorporated into body protein stores; iodide is nearly completely absorbed.

biomarkers.why: The most sensitive functional readout of thyroid status; it **rises in deficiency/hypothyroidism** and falls with excess.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then ~6–12 weeks after any change

biomarkers.why: Direct measure of circulating thyroid hormone, useful alongside TSH.
Reflects **recent iodine intake**; a single spot value is noisy for an individual and is really a population marker.
Tracks selenium status; **glutathione peroxidase activity plateaus** around 90–100 µg/L, so higher adds little — serum >150 µg/L suggests excess intake.
biomarkers.range: ~90–120 µg/L is ample; chronic >400–500 µg/L raises toxicity risk

biomarkers.why: Flag **autoimmune thyroiditis**; positive titres change how iodine should be handled, since excess can worsen it.
Iron gates thyroid peroxidase; **low ferritin can blunt the response to iodine** even when intake is adequate.
biomarkers.when: If TSH stays high despite adequate intake

needs.role: **Deiodinase** enzymes that convert T4 to active T3 are selenoproteins; iodine is poorly used without adequate selenium.
Thyroid peroxidase is **iron-dependent**; low ferritin limits iodine incorporation into hormone.
Supports **deiodinase** activity and thyroid hormone signalling.
Supports **iodine uptake** and thyroid hormone receptor signalling.

antagonists.role: Modestly **compete with iodine uptake**; cooking reduces the effect and it mainly matters when iodine is already low.

foodFirst.line: **Iodized salt**, dairy, eggs, seafood and seaweed cover iodine; **Brazil nuts** (1–2/day), seafood and eggs cover selenium — most people meet needs from food without pills.
foodFirst.note: Brazil-nut selenium varies hugely with soil, so a few nuts can range from trivial to near the upper limit — don't stack them daily.

cost.math: Iodized salt and a couple of Brazil nuts cost cents a day; plain iodide/selenium tablets are also inexpensive.
cost.note: Food-first is essentially free here — supplements rarely justify themselves unless a deficiency is documented.

dosing.cap: Adult tolerable upper limits ≈ 1,100 µg/day iodine and ≈ 400 µg/day selenium (all sources)
dosing.note: **RDA ≈ 150 µg iodine and ≈ 55 µg selenium** for adults (more iodine in pregnancy/lactation). The dose–response is **U-shaped** — both deficiency and excess harm the thyroid, so aim for sufficiency, not megadoses.

timing.line: No strict timing; take **with food**. Separate any mineral supplement from thyroid-hormone medication by ~4 hours to avoid impairing its absorption.

cycling.line: Not cycled at nutritional doses. **High-dose iodine** (kelp megadoses, Lugol's) should not be taken chronically — it can trigger thyroid dysfunction.

contra.flag: Existing thyroid disease (Hashimoto's, Graves', nodular goitre)
contra.advice: Iodine can precipitate hypo- or hyperthyroidism in these; change intake only under a physician.
Excess iodine can worsen autoimmune damage; correct selenium and keep iodine near the RDA, not megadosed.
Loading iodine while selenium is low may increase **oxidative thyroid stress** — ensure selenium sufficiency alongside iodine.

contra.flag: Selenium excess / supplementation in the already-replete
contra.advice: Chronic intake near/above the 400 µg/day upper limit risks **selenosis** (brittle hair/nails, GI upset, neuropathy); separately, routine supplementation (~200 µg/day) in people who are already replete has been linked to higher **type-2-diabetes** risk.
Iodine needs rise (~220–290 µg/day incl. lactation) but excess is also harmful; use a prenatal-appropriate amount rather than self-dosing high iodine.

quality.line: Prefer **selenomethionine** and a defined iodine dose (potassium iodide); avoid **raw kelp** products whose iodine content is variable and often above the upper limit. Look for USP/NSF third-party testing.

nonResponders.line: If TSH or symptoms don't improve on adequate iodine and selenium, suspect **iron deficiency** (check ferritin), autoimmune thyroiditis, or a non-nutritional cause — more iodine won't help and may harm.

synergy.why: Selenium **deiodinases** convert T4 to active T3, and **glutathione peroxidase** shields the gland from the peroxide made during iodine organification — the two are functionally interdependent.
Iron-dependent **thyroid peroxidase** incorporates iodine into hormone; correcting iron improves the iodine response.
Supports **deiodinase** activity and thyroid hormone signalling.

form.buy: **Niacinamide (nicotinamide)** raises NAD+ without the flush; **immediate-release nicotinic acid** is the form used when the flush or lipid effect is actually wanted.
form.avoid: **"No-flush" niacin (inositol hexanicotinate)** is poorly hydrolysed and does not reliably raise NAD+ or move lipids; **sustained/extended-release** gram-level forms carry higher hepatotoxicity risk.
form.withFood: Take **with food** to blunt GI upset; the nicotinic-acid flush is also milder with food and worse on an empty stomach.
form.bioavailability: Both niacin and niacinamide are **well absorbed orally** and converted to NAD+ in tissues.

biomarkers.why: High-dose niacin (>1 g/day), especially sustained-release, can be **hepatotoxic**.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and periodically if using gram-level doses

biomarkers.why: Pharmacologic nicotinic acid can **raise fasting glucose and worsen insulin sensitivity**.
Niacin **competes with uric acid for renal excretion** and can precipitate gout.
biomarkers.when: If gout-prone, before starting gram-level doses

needs.role: Dietary precursor — roughly 60 mg tryptophan yields ~1 mg niacin equivalent endogenously.
Required cofactors for synthesising niacin from tryptophan; deficiency limits endogenous supply.

depletes.role: High-dose niacin/niacinamide is cleared by **N-methylation**, consuming methyl groups and potentially nudging homocysteine up.

foodFirst.line: Niacin equivalents come from **meat, fish, poultry, peanuts, legumes and whole grains**, plus tryptophan conversion; frank deficiency (pellagra) is rare on a mixed diet.
foodFirst.note: RDA is ~16 mg NE/day (men) and 14 mg (women) — easily met by ordinary food.

cost.math: A bottle of 100 x 500 mg niacinamide runs about SGD 8–20.
cost.note: One of the cheapest supplements available; price is not a reason to reach for exotic NAD+ precursors.

dosing.cap: Supplemental **UL for nicotinic acid is 35 mg/day** (set on flushing); gram-level 'lipid' dosing (1–2 g) is pharmacologic and belongs under medical supervision.
dosing.note: Nutritional need is tiny (~16 mg NE); most general-health use sits at 20–100 mg. No per-kg dosing.

timing.line: Take **with a meal**; if using nicotinic acid, avoid dosing right before bed or alongside alcohol/hot drinks, which intensify the flush.

contra.advice: Avoid gram-level and sustained-release forms.
Niacin raises uric acid and can trigger a flare.
High-dose nicotinic acid can worsen glycemic control — monitor glucose.
Nicotinic acid may aggravate ulcers; avoid high doses.

quality.line: Choose **immediate-release, third-party-tested** (USP/NSF) niacin or niacinamide; avoid proprietary 'no-flush' blends.

nonResponders.line: **Flush tachyphylaxis** — the nicotinic-acid flush usually fades over 1–2 weeks of consistent dosing; if it persists and is unwanted, switch to niacinamide, which doesn't flush.

synergy.why: Blocks the prostaglandin-mediated **flush** from nicotinic acid.
Offset the methyl-group drain of high-dose niacin and help keep homocysteine in check.

form.buy: Prefer **ecdysterone (20-hydroxyecdysone) standardised to a stated % ecdysteroid content** with a third-party certificate of analysis — the actual 20E dose matters far more than milligrams of raw 'turkesterone' powder.
form.avoid: Avoid products that list only 'Ajuga turkestanica extract' or a 'proprietary blend' with no ecdysteroid % — independent assays have repeatedly found little or no actual ecdysteroids inside.
form.bioavailability: **Oral bioavailability is very low** (around 1% in rat data) and 20E clears the body within a couple of days — a core reason human effects are modest and unproven.

foodFirst.line: Dietary ecdysteroids do occur in food — **quinoa is the richest common source, spinach much lower** — but there is no evidence eating them produces an anabolic effect, so the 'Popeye/spinach' framing is marketing, not mechanism.
foodFirst.note: Spend the effort on proven levers first: protein ~1.6-2.2 g/kg/day plus progressive resistance training drive the outcomes ecdysteroids are credited with.

cost.math: roughly SGD $40-70 for a 30-60 day supply at ~500 mg/day (rough estimate)
cost.note: You are mostly paying for an unproven ingredient — the same money on creatine or protein has far stronger evidence.

dosing.cap: no established safe upper limit
dosing.note: The one suggestive human study delivered only ~40-50 mg/day of actual ecdysterone (from capsules labelled far higher); common commercial label doses are 500-1000 mg/day but true ecdysteroid content varies wildly and is often well below label. No per-kg dosing is validated.

timing.line: Split into 2 doses across the day given rapid clearance; there is no evidence that timing changes outcomes.

cycling.line: No human data on cycling for efficacy or safety; anecdotal 8-12 week blocks are convention, not evidence — treat any long-term use as unstudied.

contra.advice: **WADA has placed ecdysterone on its Monitoring Program** (since 2020) as a possible future prohibition — athletes under anti-doping rules should avoid it.
No human safety data — avoid.

contra.flag: Treating it as a hormonal anabolic
contra.advice: Despite marketing, ecdysterone does not bind the androgen receptor (its proposed action is via estrogen receptor beta); do not use, dose, or stack it as if it were a hormonal agent.

quality.line: **Label accuracy is the single biggest issue**: independent lab tests of commercial turkesterone/ecdysterone products have frequently found ecdysteroid content far below label or effectively absent — insist on a batch certificate of analysis.

nonResponders.line: Human evidence rests on essentially one suggestive study, so a large share of users should expect no measurable effect beyond what training and diet already provide.

synergy.why: The only supportive human study showed strength and body-composition changes only in training participants — without a training stimulus there is no plausible benefit.
Any hypertrophy outcome depends on sufficient protein and a non-deficit diet; ecdysteroids cannot compensate for either being missing.

form.buy: **Fermented CS-4 mycelium** or **Cordyceps militaris fruiting-body extract** with a stated cordycepin/adenosine content and third-party heavy-metal testing.
form.avoid: **"Wild Cordyceps sinensis"** claims (expensive, frequently adulterated) and cheap **mycelium-grown-on-grain** powders that are mostly starch with negligible active content.
form.withFood: Take with or without food; a hot-water extract is fine either way.
form.bioavailability: **Hot-water/dual extraction** liberates the active compounds better than raw ground powder.

cost.math: A month of a decent CS-4 or militaris extract runs roughly SGD 25-45.
cost.note: Rough range only; genuine militaris fruiting-body extract sits at the higher end.

dosing.cap: 4-4.5 g/day
dosing.note: Human endurance studies used **1-4 g/day** of fermented CS-4 or Cordyceps militaris extract/blends; no per-kg dosing is established. Split doses are fine.

timing.line: Benefit is **chronic, not acute** — the small VO2/endurance signals appeared only after ~2-6 weeks of daily dosing, so take it daily rather than as a pre-workout.

cycling.line: No cycling required; it can be taken continuously and there is no established tolerance or dependence.

quality.line: Cordyceps (especially wild sinensis) can **concentrate arsenic and other heavy metals**, so choose products with published third-party heavy-metal results; also confirm a stated active content since grain-grown mycelium is often near-inert.

nonResponders.line: The endurance effect is **modest and inconsistent** — several trials in already-trained athletes showed little or no VO2max change, so a well-conditioned user may notice nothing.

contra.flag: Anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy / upcoming surgery
contra.advice: Cordyceps may have **antiplatelet effects**; discuss with a doctor and consider pausing before surgery.

contra.flag: Autoimmune disease or immunosuppressant therapy (e.g. transplant)
contra.advice: It is **immunomodulatory** in models, which could theoretically interfere with immunosuppression or autoimmune control — get clinician sign-off.
May **lower blood glucose** in animal models; monitor for additive hypoglycaemia.
