Insufficient safety data — **avoid**.

synergy.why: The two are often combined in endurance research blends; both are proposed anti-fatigue adaptogens, though the isolated contribution of each is unclear.

form.buy: **Agmatine sulfate** and **theacrine** as high-purity, third-party-tested powders or capsules; for **glycerol**, hyperhydration-grade pure glycerol (or hydrating-glycerol powder) rather than the low-yield glycerol monostearate (GMS) used only for topical pump feel.
form.avoid: GMS labelled as 'glycerol' when your goal is hyperhydration — it is only ~25% glycerol by weight, so the actual glycerol dose is a fraction of what's on the tub.
form.withFood: Theacrine works fine on an empty stomach; glycerol must be taken with a large, deliberate fluid load (and ideally sodium) or it does nothing useful and can cause GI upset.
form.bioavailability: Theacrine is orally well-absorbed with a long half-life (~16-26 h); glycerol's benefit depends entirely on co-ingested fluid volume, not the glycerol dose alone.

biomarkers.why: Theacrine is a caffeine-like stimulant; track these at home when adding it or stacking it with caffeine to catch excess cardiovascular load.
biomarkers.range: Resting HR ~60-100 bpm; BP <120/80 mmHg is normal, persistently >=130/80 warrants caution
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then a few times in the first weeks and after any dose increase

biomarkers.why: For glycerol hyperhydration, a ~1-1.5 kg gain after the loading protocol confirms fluid was actually retained; no gain means the protocol failed.
biomarkers.range: Expect roughly +1 to +1.5 L (kg) of retained fluid with a proper glycerol + fluid protocol
biomarkers.when: Before and 2-3 h after the loading dose

needs.role: Glycerol only produces hyperhydration when taken with ~25-30 mL/kg of fluid; without the water load it is inert for performance.
Co-ingesting sodium with glycerol increases the fluid volume retained and reduces urine loss.

foodFirst.line: For the pump/NO goal that **agmatine** targets, dietary **nitrate from beetroot juice or leafy greens** has far stronger evidence for raising nitric oxide and blood flow.
foodFirst.note: Agmatine's NO/pump claims in humans are weak and largely mechanistic; food-source nitrate and dietary L-citrulline (watermelon) are better-supported first moves.

cost.math: Bulk agmatine sulfate and glycerol are cheap (cents per gram); theacrine is the pricier ingredient at roughly SGD $0.30-0.70 per 100-200 mg serving.
cost.note: Rough Singapore ballpark for bulk powders; capsules and pre-mixed 'pump' blends cost several times more per dose.

dosing.cap: ~1.2-1.4 g/kg glycerol for hyperhydration (~85-100 g for a 70 kg person)
dosing.note: Per-kg dosing applies ONLY to glycerol hyperhydration (~1.2-1.4 g/kg with ~25-30 mL/kg fluid). Agmatine sulfate is dosed flat at ~500-1000 mg (max ~2000 mg/day); theacrine flat at ~100-300 mg (treat as a stimulant load and cap total daily stimulants).

timing.line: Agmatine and theacrine ~30-60 min pre-workout; glycerol + fluid loaded over ~1-3 h before exercise. Theacrine's long half-life means avoid late-afternoon or evening dosing to protect sleep.

cycling.line: Theacrine shows little tolerance build-up over ~8 weeks of daily use (unlike caffeine), so aggressive cycling is less necessary — but treating it like any stimulant with periodic breaks is still sensible. Glycerol and agmatine are used situationally, not continuously.

contra.advice: Theacrine is a stimulant — avoid or clear with a clinician, and count it toward your total daily stimulant/caffeine load rather than adding on top.
Rapid glycerol fluid-loading stresses fluid handling and commonly causes headache, nausea, or GI distress; start low and avoid if renal function is compromised.
Glycerol was removed from the WADA prohibited list in 2018, but verify the current status of any stimulant blend against your governing body before competition.

quality.line: Choose powders/capsules with third-party testing (e.g. NSF, Informed Sport for tested athletes) since minor pre-workout ingredients are frequently under-dosed, mislabelled, or spiked with unlisted stimulants.

nonResponders.line: Many people feel nothing from agmatine — its subjective pump effect is unproven and easily confused with placebo. Some also find theacrine noticeably milder than an equivalent caffeine dose.

synergy.why: Co-ingesting caffeine raises theacrine's plasma exposure (Cmax and AUC) without changing caffeine's own levels — the most consistent human finding — and the pairing is used for a smoother, longer stimulant effect.
Added to a glycerol + fluid load, sodium and creatine each increase total body water retained, improving the hyperhydration goal.
If chasing a pump, these have better human NO/blood-flow evidence than agmatine and are a more rational stack than agmatine alone.

form.buy: High-purity **yeast-derived (1,3)/(1,6)-beta-glucan** (from *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) or mushroom-sourced material — this branched linkage is what engages dectin-1 and drives the trained-immunity effect.
form.avoid: **Oat/barley (1,3)/(1,4)-beta-glucan** if your goal is immune priming — that soluble fiber lowers cholesterol and blood glucose but does not prime innate cells the same way.
form.withFood: Take **with or without food** — timing evidence is thin, so consistency matters more than clock time.
form.bioavailability: Not absorbed intact into blood — particulate beta-glucan is **sampled by gut-associated immune cells (M cells, macrophages)**, so 'blood levels' are not the mechanism and don't need chasing.

foodFirst.line: Eat **mushrooms regularly** — shiitake, maitake, oyster — for background (1,3)/(1,6)-beta-glucan intake alongside their other nutrients.
foodFirst.note: Food amounts of immune-active glucan are modest and rarely match the 100-500 mg standardized doses used in prevention trials.

cost.math: 250 mg/day standardized yeast beta-glucan ≈ SGD 15-30/month
cost.note: Rough SG retail range; mushroom-blend products vary widely.

dosing.cap: 100-500 mg/day; **250 mg/day** is the most-studied immune dose
dosing.note: No validated per-kg dosing. Effect builds over weeks of daily use — it is not an acute, dose-on-symptom compound.

timing.line: Take **daily and consistently** — trained-immunity benefits accrue over roughly 4-12 weeks, not from single doses.

cycling.line: Many use it **seasonally** during higher-risk windows (cold/flu season, heavy training blocks); continuous daily use is also well tolerated.

contra.advice: Beta-glucan stimulates innate immunity via dectin-1 — check with a clinician before use if you have an autoimmune disease or take immunosuppressive medication.
Choose mushroom-sourced beta-glucan rather than *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*-derived product.
Insufficient safety data — avoid unless cleared by a clinician.

quality.line: Pick products that **specify the glucan linkage and source** and are third-party tested for purity; independent standards (NSF, Informed Sport) add assurance against contamination.

nonResponders.line: Benefit is clearest in people **prone to upper-respiratory infections or under high physical/psychological stress** (e.g. athletes in heavy training); healthy low-risk individuals may notice little.

synergy.why: Corrects a common deficiency that independently supports immune defense, complementing beta-glucan's innate-immune priming.
Supports immune-cell function and can shorten colds; a logical pairing for URTI prevention.
Modestly reduces cold duration/severity in physically stressed people; low-risk, cheap pairing.

form.buy: **Fruiting-body extract**, hot-water extracted and standardized to a stated **beta-glucan %** (reishi benefits from dual water+alcohol extraction to also capture triterpenes).
form.avoid: **Mycelium-grown-on-grain** products and labels that only list **'polysaccharides %'** — that number counts grain starch (alpha-glucans), not the active 1,3/1,6-beta-glucans.
form.bioavailability: Beta-glucans are locked in a tough **chitin-rich cell wall**; hot-water extraction breaks that matrix down to make them soluble, whereas raw dried powder and grain-based mycelium are largely indigestible fiber and starch.

dosing.cap: Reishi human studies used up to ~9 g/day dried-equivalent; no established need to exceed that.
dosing.note: General immune use: roughly **1-3 g/day of extract**. Turkey tail trials used **~3 g/day of PSK/PSP**. There is no validated per-kg dosing — dose by product beta-glucan content, not body weight.

timing.line: Anytime for turkey tail and chaga; many take **reishi in the evening** since it is used traditionally for calm and sleep quality.

cycling.line: Generally used **continuously** — benefits are cumulative/preventive rather than acute, so daily dosing is standard and no washout is established.

foodFirst.line: **Culinary mushrooms** (shiitake, maitake, oyster) supply the same 1,3/1,6-beta-glucans and belong in the diet first; reishi, chaga and turkey tail are too woody to eat and only useful as extracts.
foodFirst.note: Variety matters more than dose here — a regular mixed-mushroom intake covers most of the general immune rationale.

cost.math: A month of a decent fruiting-body extract runs roughly SGD 30-60, i.e. about SGD 1-2/day.
cost.note: Grain-mycelium powders are cheaper per gram but deliver far less beta-glucan, so cost-per-active is often worse.

contra.advice: **Reishi** can increase bleeding time and has mild antiplatelet activity; combine with blood thinners only under medical supervision and pause before surgery.

contra.flag: Autoimmune disease, transplant, or immunosuppressant therapy
contra.advice: These mushrooms **modulate immune activity**; the interaction is theoretical but real enough to clear with your physician before use.

contra.flag: Kidney stones / kidney disease and heavy chaga use
contra.advice: **Chaga is exceptionally high in oxalates** and case reports link heavy intake (~10-15 g/day powder) to oxalate nephropathy — avoid or strictly limit chaga if you have renal risk.
Safety data are insufficient — best avoided.

quality.line: Mushrooms **bioaccumulate heavy metals** (chaga especially) — buy third-party-tested extracts (heavy metals + a quantified beta-glucan assay), ideally under a standard like **NSF** or **USP**.

biomarkers.why: Only relevant for regular **chaga** users — its high oxalate load has been linked to oxalate nephropathy; watch renal function if using chaga long-term or with kidney risk.
biomarkers.range: eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m^2; creatinine within lab reference
biomarkers.when: Baseline and periodically during sustained chaga use

nonResponders.line: General immune effects are **subclinical** — you should not expect to 'feel' anything beyond, for some people, reishi's calming effect. Absence of a sensation is not non-response.

form.buy: Elderberry standardized to **anthocyanins** (and species-verified *Sambucus nigra*); andrographis standardized to **andrographolide** (% or mg per dose stated); bovine lactoferrin and colostrum standardized to protein / **IgG** content.
form.avoid: **Raw or unripe elderberry**, bark, leaves and seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides that cause nausea and vomiting — only cooked berries or finished extracts are safe.
form.withFood: Take andrographis **with food** to blunt its bitterness and GI upset; elderberry, lactoferrin and colostrum are flexible.
form.bioavailability: Lactoferrin and colostrum are proteins that are partly broken down in the gut, so studied benefits use fairly large daily doses rather than trace food amounts.

biomarkers.why: Lactoferrin is an iron-binding protein studied in iron-deficiency states (including pregnancy); if you use it around iron status, track the effect rather than assume it.
biomarkers.range: Ferritin normal ~30-300 ng/mL; functional deficiency often flagged <30 (WHO uses <15)
biomarkers.when: Baseline and after ~8-12 weeks of daily lactoferrin

antagonists.role: Lactoferrin binds iron; separate it from iron tablets by a couple of hours so neither saturates nor blocks the other.

foodFirst.line: Sleep, hand-washing and a **vitamin-C / zinc-adequate diet** prevent and blunt colds more reliably than any of these — treat the supplements as duration/severity shavers, not a substitute.
foodFirst.note: Culinary elderberry must be cooked, and ordinary dairy has only trace lactoferrin/colostrum; therapeutic doses come only from concentrates.

cost.perDose: Elderberry & andrographis ~S$0.30-1 per dose; lactoferrin & colostrum ~S$1-3 per day
cost.note: Lactoferrin and colostrum are the expensive members of this cluster; elderberry syrups cost more per gram of actives than capsules.

dosing.note: No per-kg dosing exists. Elderberry and andrographis are dosed to their standardized actives (e.g. andrographolide) and started at symptom onset in 3-4 divided daily doses; lactoferrin and colostrum are taken as fixed daily grams/hundreds of mg. Follow the standardized label rather than raw extract weight.

timing.line: Elderberry and andrographis are **acute tools** — start within 24-48h of first symptoms, split across the day, and take andrographis with food. Lactoferrin and colostrum are **daily**, best on an empty stomach and away from iron supplements.

cycling.line: Run elderberry and andrographis as **short acute courses** (through the cold, ~5-10 days) rather than year-round — both are immunostimulants with limited long-term-use data. Lactoferrin and colostrum can be taken continuously.

contra.flag: Autoimmune disease, transplant, or immunosuppressant drugs
contra.advice: **Elderberry and andrographis stimulate immune activity** in lab studies — avoid or clear with your doctor to prevent counteracting therapy.
**Avoid andrographis** — WHO contraindicates it in pregnancy and animal data raise reproductive-toxicity concerns; safety of the others in pregnancy is also not established.

contra.flag: Blood thinners, blood-pressure or glucose-lowering medication
contra.advice: **Andrographis may add to these effects** (antiplatelet/hypotensive signals, mostly preclinical) — monitor closely and involve your prescriber.
**Lactoferrin and colostrum are bovine dairy proteins** — avoid them.

quality.line: Elderberry is one of the **most adulterated** botanicals (cut with black-rice extract or dye) — insist on species-verified, anthocyanin-standardized, third-party-tested product; NSF or USP marks are reasonable neutral signals.

nonResponders.line: Elderberry's cold-duration evidence rests on small, largely industry-funded trials with mixed replication (a Cleveland Clinic influenza RCT found no benefit); if an honest trial shortens nothing for you, it probably won't.

synergy.why: Zinc lozenges started early independently shorten cold duration — the best-evidenced acute cold tool to pair with these.
Correcting deficiency modestly lowers respiratory-infection risk; a sensible foundation under any acute immune supplement.
Lactoferrin and colostrum act on gut immunity, so pairing them with probiotics is a coherent gut-immune stack.

form.buy: Choose **niacinamide (nicotinamide)**, not niacin/nicotinic acid, to avoid the harmless but uncomfortable flush; useful topical strengths are **4-5% niacinamide** serums and **10% azelaic acid** (OTC/cosmetic).
form.avoid: Skip **high-dose niacin (nicotinic acid)** for skin (flushing, no added benefit) and **biotin megadoses (5,000-10,000 mcg)** marketed for hair, which do nothing in non-deficient people.
form.withFood: Oral niacinamide is fine with or without food; apply topicals to clean, dry skin.
form.bioavailability: Oral niacinamide is well absorbed; prescription **15-20% azelaic acid** penetrates better than 10% OTC for stubborn acne or rosacea.

biomarkers.marker: Immunoassay labs (TSH, free T4, troponin, some hormone panels)
biomarkers.why: High-dose **biotin** interferes with biotin-based immunoassays, producing falsely high or low results and real misdiagnoses.
biomarkers.range: Hold biotin at least 2 days before testing (up to 72 hours if taking very high doses)
biomarkers.when: Before any blood panel that includes thyroid, hormones or cardiac markers

biomarkers.why: Only relevant at **gram-level oral niacinamide** (roughly >3 g/day), which can stress the liver; skin-dose oral and all topical use do not require monitoring.
biomarkers.when: Only if taking multi-gram oral doses long-term

foodFirst.line: **Vitamin B3 and biotin deficiency are both rare** - B3 is abundant in meat, fish, poultry, peanuts and whole grains, and biotin is in eggs, nuts and seeds plus what gut bacteria make.
foodFirst.note: Supplementing biotin without a true deficiency does not improve hair or nails; large amounts of raw egg white (avidin) are the main dietary way to actually deplete biotin.

cost.perDose: Oral niacinamide ~SGD 0.05-0.15/tablet; a tube of topical 10% azelaic acid ~SGD 20-40 (rough)
cost.note: Topical niacinamide and azelaic acid are the value plays; branded 'hair vitamin' biotin megadoses are largely wasted money unless you are deficient.

dosing.note: Oral niacinamide is typically **500 mg** in skin studies; topical **4-5% niacinamide** twice daily; **azelaic acid 10% OTC** (or 15-20% Rx) as a thin layer twice daily; biotin adequate intake is only ~**30 mcg/day**, so most 'hair' doses are 100-300x higher with no proven benefit.

timing.line: Apply **azelaic acid** to clean, dry skin (mild early tingling is normal) and **use daily sunscreen** - acne-prone and actively treated skin is more sun-sensitive; niacinamide serum works equally AM or PM.

contra.advice: Stop **high-dose biotin** at least 2 days before labs (72 hours for very high doses) to avoid false thyroid, hormone or troponin readings.
Patch-test **azelaic acid** and start once daily; transient stinging is common, but persistent burning or worsening redness means scale back.
Doses far above skin-study levels (grams/day) can affect the liver and glucose - stay near studied doses and never confuse niacinamide with flushing high-dose niacin.

quality.line: Confirm the label reads **niacinamide / nicotinamide** (not niacin) and prefer **third-party-tested (USP/NSF)** oral products; azelaic acid efficacy depends on the labeled percentage, not branding.

nonResponders.line: **Biotin won't help hair or nails unless you're genuinely deficient**; topical niacinamide and azelaic acid act slowly, so judge results at **8-12 weeks**, not days.

synergy.why: **Niacinamide + zinc** are combined in some acne formulations targeting sebum and inflammation, though evidence that the pair beats either alone is limited.
**Niacinamide + azelaic acid** both calm redness and fade post-acne marks, pairing well for rosacea-prone skin.
**Niacinamide buffers retinoid irritation** and supports the barrier, improving tolerance of a retinoid routine.
**SPF** protects the tone and pigment gains from niacinamide and azelaic acid, since UV drives the hyperpigmentation they treat.

form.buy: **High-purity myo-inositol powder** in a **40:1 myo:D-chiro-inositol ratio** — this ratio mirrors the body's physiological plasma ratio and is the form used in the bulk of positive PCOS trials.
form.avoid: **D-chiro-inositol-dominant or 1:1 blends** — excess D-chiro can worsen oocyte quality and ovarian outcomes (the 'DCI paradox'). Also skip proprietary blends that hide the actual myo/DCI amounts.
form.withFood: Well tolerated either way; taking it with a meal reduces the mild GI upset some people get at the 4g dose.
form.bioavailability: **Readily absorbed orally**; tasteless-to-slightly-sweet powder dissolves in water, which is cheaper per gram than capsules for a 4g/day dose.

biomarkers.why: The primary mechanism is restoring ovarian insulin sensitivity, so insulin resistance markers are the most direct readout of whether it is working.
Lower insulin reduces ovarian androgen output; falling testosterone tracks improvement in acne and unwanted hair.
Rises as insulin falls, binding more free androgen — a useful corroborating signal alongside testosterone.
biomarkers.when: With the androgen panel at 3 months

biomarkers.why: The functional endpoint — restored ovulation and more regular cycles is what most trials measure as success.

needs.role: Commonly co-formulated with myo-inositol in PCOS/fertility trials (typically 200-400 mcg); relevant if conception is a goal, though not required for the insulin-sensitizing effect itself.

foodFirst.line: **Dietary inositol** is found in fruits, beans, whole grains and nuts, but typical intake is only ~1g/day — far below the ~4g used therapeutically.
foodFirst.note: Whole-food inositol is a baseline, not a treatment dose; you cannot eat your way to 4g/day practically.

cost.math: A 500g tub of myo-inositol powder runs ~$30-40 SGD and lasts ~125 days at 4g/day.
cost.note: One of the cheapest evidence-backed options in this category; powder beats capsules on price per gram.

dosing.cap: GI tolerance limits most people to ~12g/day; the standard PCOS dose is well below this.
dosing.note: Standard protocol is **4g myo-inositol + ~100mg D-chiro-inositol daily (40:1)**, split into two doses. No per-kg dosing — it is a fixed protocol dose, not weight-scaled.

timing.line: Split into **two ~2g doses**, morning and evening; allow **8-12 weeks** before judging effect and re-checking labs — cycle and metabolic changes are gradual.

cycling.line: **Taken continuously**, not cycled; benefits build over months and reverse if stopped, so treat it as ongoing while reassessing at 3-month intervals.

contra.advice: Widely studied and generally regarded as safe (including for gestational-diabetes risk), but confirm dose and timing with your OB/GYN rather than self-directing.
High-dose inositol has isolated reports of triggering hypomania/mania; anyone with bipolar illness should clear it with their doctor first.
Nausea, gas and loose stools appear mainly above ~12g/day; the 4g PCOS dose is usually well tolerated, and splitting doses helps.

quality.line: Choose **third-party-tested powder** verified for purity and free of undisclosed fillers; independent standards (NSF, USP) are reasonable neutral markers of manufacturing quality.

nonResponders.line: A meaningful minority (**roughly a third in some reports**) don't achieve regular ovulation on inositol alone; the myo:DCI ratio matters, and non-responders may warrant evaluation for other drivers (e.g. thyroid, adrenal, or a metformin trial with their doctor).

synergy.why: Vitamin D deficiency is common in PCOS and independently linked to insulin resistance; correcting it complements inositol's metabolic effect.
Combination trials pairing ALA with myo-inositol show added improvement in insulin sensitivity in PCOS.
Standard co-formulation in fertility-oriented protocols; supports the conception use-case rather than the insulin mechanism.

form.buy: **Standardized extracts and single, disclosed actives**: vitex standardized to casticin/agnuside, DIM in an enhanced-absorption (microencapsulated) form, and calcium-D-glucarate as the D-isomer.
form.avoid: Proprietary 'hormone balance' blends that hide individual doses, and crude high-dose I3C sold as interchangeable with DIM.
form.withFood: Take DIM with a fat-containing meal; vitex is traditionally taken in the morning; split calcium-D-glucarate across the day.
form.bioavailability: Crystalline DIM is poorly and erratically absorbed — enhanced-absorption formulations reach severalfold higher plasma levels at a lower stated dose.

biomarkers.why: DIM/I3C shift **estrogen hydroxylation** toward the 2-OH pathway, raising this ratio.
biomarkers.range: A higher ratio favors the 2-OH pathway and is generally considered more favorable; there is no universally agreed cutoff, so interpret against the specific lab's reference range.

biomarkers.why: Vitex is **dopaminergic** and can lower mildly elevated prolactin — its main proposed mechanism in PMS and cyclic mastalgia.
biomarkers.when: Baseline if cycle irregularity or breast tenderness; recheck after ~3 cycles.

biomarkers.why: Vitex may support **luteal progesterone** where it is low; check ~7 days post-ovulation.
biomarkers.range: ≥3-5 ng/mL confirms ovulation has occurred; >10 ng/mL indicates a robust luteal phase (cycle- and assay-dependent).

needs.role: Required to absorb fat-soluble DIM.
Supports dopamine synthesis and has independent PMS evidence, complementing vitex.

foodFirst.line: **Cruciferous vegetables** (broccoli, kale, cabbage, Brussels sprouts) supply the indole precursors DIM is derived from, plus sulforaphane.
foodFirst.note: A few servings a week plus ground flaxseed (lignans) covers much of what these supplements target, at food doses.

cost.math: DIM 100-200 mg ≈ $0.30-0.70; vitex standardized extract ≈ $0.10-0.30; calcium-D-glucarate 500 mg ≈ $0.30-0.60.
cost.note: Rough Singapore ranges; standardized and enhanced-absorption forms sit at the higher end.

dosing.cap: DIM is commonly kept at ≤200-300 mg/day.
dosing.note: Typical adult ranges: DIM 100-200 mg/day; calcium-D-glucarate 500-1500 mg/day in divided doses; vitex standardized extract ~20-40 mg once daily (or ~150-240 mg crude equivalent). Do not stack multiple estrogen-modulating agents without a specific reason.

timing.line: Vitex in the **morning** (aligns with its action on morning prolactin/dopamine); DIM with the largest fatty meal; calcium-D-glucarate split across the day.

cycling.line: Vitex effects build over **~3 menstrual cycles** — judge it at that point, not after a few weeks, and reassess periodically rather than taking indefinitely.

contra.advice: Avoid all three — vitex is hormonally active and DIM/calcium-D-glucarate are not established as safe.
Vitex acts on dopamine receptors — avoid alongside antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or Parkinson's dopamine agonists.
Vitex may interfere with hormonal contraceptives; discuss before combining.
These agents modify estrogen metabolism/activity — use only under oncology guidance.
DIM can turn urine orange/brownish and occasionally causes mild headache — not dangerous, but lower the dose if it bothers you.

quality.line: Prefer **third-party tested** products (NSF, USP, Informed Choice) and vitex extracts that state casticin/agnuside standardization; avoid undisclosed proprietary blends.

nonResponders.line: Vitex mainly helps **prolactin-driven / luteal** PMS — if symptoms don't arise from that pathway it may do nothing even after 3 cycles; DIM's effect depends heavily on baseline estrogen metabolism.

synergy.why: CDG inhibits β-glucuronidase to reduce estrogen reabsorption while DIM shifts hydroxylation — complementary points on estrogen clearance, though CDG's human outcome data are largely preclinical.
Adds Nrf2-driven phase II detox support alongside DIM's shift in estrogen metabolism.
Both have independent PMS evidence and pair well with vitex for cyclical symptoms.

form.buy: Two useful forms: **calcium citrate** (absorbs with or without food, better if older or on acid-reducing drugs) and **calcium carbonate** (cheaper, ~40% elemental calcium, needs food/stomach acid). Pair with **D3 (cholecalciferol)** and **K2 as MK-7**.
form.avoid: Avoid **bone meal, oyster shell, dolomite, and coral calcium** — unrefined mineral sources that have historically carried lead. Skip **D2 (ergocalciferol)** where D3 is available.
form.withFood: Carbonate with meals; citrate any time. Take **≤500 mg elemental calcium per dose** — the absorbed fraction drops above that.
form.bioavailability: Read the **elemental calcium** figure, not tablet weight: carbonate ~40%, citrate ~21%. Citrate absorbs better on an empty stomach and with low stomach acid.

biomarkers.why: Vitamin D status gates calcium absorption; if it's low, supplementing calcium alone does little.
biomarkers.range: 75-125 nmol/L (30-50 ng/mL) optimal; sufficiency ≥50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then ~3 months after starting D3

biomarkers.why: Safety check for hypercalcemia; flags over-supplementation or an undiagnosed parathyroid problem.
biomarkers.when: Baseline; recheck if dosing high-dose D3

biomarkers.why: High PTH with low-normal calcium suggests inadequate calcium/D; high PTH with high calcium flags hyperparathyroidism.
The actual outcome you're optimizing for bone health.

needs.role: Drives active intestinal calcium absorption; without adequate D, most supplemental calcium isn't absorbed.
Activates osteocalcin and matrix Gla protein, directing calcium into bone and away from arterial walls.
Cofactor for converting vitamin D to its active form and a structural component of the bone matrix.

antagonists.role: Calcium blocks non-heme iron absorption; separate the two by 2+ hours.
Calcium binds it and reduces absorption; separate by at least 4 hours.
Calcium chelates these antibiotics; separate dosing by 2-4 hours.

foodFirst.line: **Food-first beats pills for calcium**: dairy, calcium-set tofu, canned sardines/salmon with bones, and leafy greens (kale, bok choy) deliver calcium in a food matrix not linked to the cardiovascular signal seen with high-dose supplements.
foodFirst.note: One cup of milk/yogurt ≈ 300 mg; 100 g sardines with bones ≈ 350 mg. Most people reach target with 2-3 servings/day and only need supplements to fill the gap.

cost.math: A bottle of calcium+D3 tablets runs ~$10-25 SGD for 60-100 doses; standalone K2 (MK-7) ~$20-40 for 2-3 months.
cost.note: Dietary calcium from dairy or canned fish is often cheaper per gram and better evidenced than pills.

dosing.cap: Total intake (food + supplements) under ~2000-2500 mg/day; the supplemental portion is usually 500-600 mg to fill the dietary gap, not another 1000+.
dosing.note: Adult target ~1000 mg/day total (1200 mg for women 50+ and men 70+). Supplement only the shortfall after counting food. D3 ~800-2000 IU/day; K2 as MK-7 ~90-180 mcg/day. No per-kg dosing.

timing.line: Split into **≤500 mg elemental doses** with meals; take at a different time from iron, thyroid meds, and certain antibiotics. Take fat-soluble **K2 and D3 with the largest fatty meal**.

cycling.line: Not cycled — calcium/D3/K2 are taken continuously to maintain status; there's no tolerance or benefit to breaks.

contra.advice: High-dose calcium *supplements* may raise stone risk while dietary calcium lowers it — prioritize food, take supplements with meals, stay well hydrated, and discuss with your doctor.
Do not supplement calcium/D without medical supervision — you can worsen dangerously high calcium.
Some analyses link high-dose calcium supplements (not dietary calcium) to modest CV risk; food-first, adequate K2, and modest supplemental doses mitigate this.
Calcium and vitamin D handling is altered — dosing must be individualized by a nephrologist.
These raise hypercalcemia risk when combined with calcium/D; monitor serum calcium.

quality.line: Choose products **third-party tested (USP, NSF)** and avoid unrefined mineral sources (bone meal, oyster shell, dolomite, coral) that have historically carried lead. Confirm the **elemental calcium** figure on the label.

nonResponders.line: If DEXA doesn't improve or PTH stays high, check **vitamin D status, magnesium, stomach acid (chronic PPI use), and malabsorption (celiac)** — calcium can't work without absorption and adequate D.

synergy.why: Increases the fraction of dietary and supplemental calcium actually absorbed in the gut.
Carboxylates osteocalcin and MGP to route calcium into bone and help inhibit arterial calcification.
Needed to activate vitamin D and build bone matrix; deficiency blunts the whole system.
Mechanical loading and protein are the primary drivers of bone remodeling; calcium is the raw material they build with.

form.buy: Strontium as **strontium citrate** (OTC); silicon as **orthosilicic acid** or choline-stabilized orthosilicic acid (ch-OSA), or bamboo/horsetail-derived silica. Choose high-purity, third-party-tested products (heavy-metal screened).
form.avoid: **Strontium ranelate** is a withdrawn/prescription-restricted drug carrying cardiovascular and venous-thromboembolism warnings — not a self-directed supplement. Avoid megadose strontium.
form.withFood: Take **strontium away from calcium and meals** — calcium sharply reduces strontium uptake. Silicon (orthosilicic acid) is absorbed with or without food.
form.bioavailability: Strontium absorption is **reduced by dietary calcium and food**; orthosilicic acid is the most bioavailable silicon form (polymerized/colloidal silica absorbs poorly).

biomarkers.why: The standard bone-density track, but strontium's high atomic number attenuates X-rays more than calcium, so it **artificially inflates measured BMD** — always tell the technician and physician you take it so readings can be interpreted cautiously.
biomarkers.range: T-score above -1.0 normal; -1.0 to -2.5 osteopenia; below -2.5 osteoporosis

biomarkers.why: Bone-mineral optimization is pointless without adequate vitamin D driving calcium handling.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, recheck after correcting a deficiency

biomarkers.why: Baseline calcium status guides whether calcium/D need correcting before adding bone minerals.

needs.role: Enables intestinal calcium absorption and normal bone mineralization
Activates osteocalcin, helping direct mineral into bone matrix
Primary bone mineral; adequacy is the foundation any strontium/silica sits on
Cofactor for bone matrix and vitamin D metabolism

antagonists.role: Competes with strontium for the same intestinal transport — co-dosing blunts strontium absorption, so separate them

foodFirst.line: **Silicon is abundant in ordinary diet** — oats and whole grains, bananas, green beans, leafy greens, beer, and silica-rich mineral water; typical intakes of 20-50 mg/day already meet most needs.
foodFirst.note: Strontium is not a food-first target at supplemental doses, but trace amounts occur in seafood and some mineral waters.

cost.math: Strontium citrate ~$20-35 SGD/month plus a silica supplement ~$15-30 SGD/month
cost.note: Rough Singapore online/pharmacy ranges; dietary silicon is effectively free.

dosing.cap: 680 mg elemental strontium/day
dosing.note: Strontium: bone trials used ~680 mg elemental strontium (as ranelate, from a 2 g salt dose) daily — do not exceed this with citrate. Silicon (as ch-OSA): ~6-12 mg elemental silicon/day is the studied range. Per-kg dosing does not apply.

timing.line: Take **strontium at night, several hours from any calcium** (supplement or dairy meal) to limit absorption competition; silicon can be taken any time.

contra.advice: Strontium ranelate was restricted for **increased heart attack and venous-thromboembolism risk** — anyone with ischemic heart disease, prior clots, or uncontrolled hypertension should avoid supplemental strontium and speak to a doctor.
Strontium is renally cleared and accumulates in impaired kidneys — avoid without medical supervision.
Strontium **inflates DEXA density readings**, overstating true bone gain — always disclose use so results aren't misinterpreted as real mineral gain.
Strontium crosses into bone and placenta and is not established as safe — avoid.

quality.line: Prefer products with **third-party heavy-metal testing** (strontium mineral sources can carry contaminants) and independent certification (USP/NSF) where available.

nonResponders.line: A large DEXA 'improvement' on strontium is frequently **partly an artifact** of strontium's X-ray attenuation, not true new bone — apparent responders may be overstated; judge outcomes by fracture history and other markers, not BMD alone.

synergy.why: Supply the absorption and matrix-activation steps that any bone mineral depends on
Mechanical loading is the strongest non-drug osteogenic stimulus and complements mineral support
Vitamin C is a required cofactor for collagen synthesis, the connective-tissue matrix silicon supports

form.buy: For **ginkgo**, use extract standardized to **24% flavone glycosides + 6% terpene lactones** (the classic pharmacopoeial spec); for **ginseng**, standardized ginsenoside content; for **uridine**, the **uridine-5'-monophosphate (UMP)** form.
form.avoid: Crude, unstandardized ginkgo leaf powder (variable actives, higher **ginkgolic acid** load) and huperzine A products dosed far above the microgram range.
form.withFood: **UMP** is often taken sublingually in nootropic practice, on the rationale of bypassing first-pass metabolism, though the evidence for it being necessary is thin; ginseng and ginkgo can be taken with or without food.
form.bioavailability: Dietary/oral **uridine** is largely metabolized in the gut and liver, so the **UMP** form is used to raise plasma uridine; huperzine A is a small molecule that crosses the blood-brain barrier well.

needs.role: Substrate for acetylcholine — complements the cholinergic action of huperzine A and uridine.
Partners with uridine to build neuronal membrane phospholipids via the Kennedy pathway.

foodFirst.line: Cover **choline** from food first — **eggs, liver, and soy** are rich sources — before layering on cholinergic nootropics.
foodFirst.note: A solid dietary choline base makes acetylcholine-oriented compounds like huperzine A and uridine more sensible.

dosing.note: Typical self-directed daily ranges: **huperzine A 50-200 mcg**, **ginkgo 120-240 mg** standardized extract, **panax ginseng 200-400 mg** standardized extract, **UMP 150-300 mg**. Start at the low end of each.
dosing.cap: For self-directed use, **huperzine A ~200 mcg/day** is a sensible ceiling; higher doses used in dementia trials (up to 400 mcg twice daily) produce dose-dependent cholinergic side effects (nausea, cramps, vivid dreams) and are not for casual self-administration.

timing.line: Take **ginseng and huperzine A in the morning** — ginseng is mildly stimulating and huperzine's cholinergic effect is long-lasting, so late dosing can disrupt sleep or trigger vivid dreams. Uridine is timing-neutral.

cycling.line: Some users **cycle huperzine A** (e.g. 2-3 weeks on then a break, or 5-days-on/2-off) on the theory that continuous acetylcholinesterase inhibition may blunt response, though this is largely anecdotal; ginseng is also traditionally taken with periodic breaks.

contra.advice: **Ginkgo** may add to bleeding risk — avoid combining and stop at least 1-2 weeks before any procedure.

contra.flag: On cholinesterase-inhibitor or other cholinergic drugs
contra.advice: **Huperzine A** stacks with these effects; also use caution with asthma, slow heart rate, ulcers, or a seizure disorder given its cholinergic activity.
**Panax ginseng** can lower blood glucose and may shift blood pressure; it can also alter warfarin's effect (reports are mixed) — monitor and coordinate with your clinician.
**Ginkgo** may lower the seizure threshold, especially crude products contaminated with **ginkgotoxin** — best avoided.

quality.line: For **ginkgo**, look for **ginkgolic acid <5 ppm** on the certificate of analysis; **ginseng** is a frequently adulterated botanical, so favor **third-party-tested** (USP / NSF) material.

nonResponders.line: **Ginkgo's** cognitive effects are modest and build over weeks rather than acute — feeling nothing on a given dose is expected, and a subset of people see no measurable benefit at all.

synergy.with: A choline source (citicoline / alpha-GPC or dietary choline)
synergy.why: **Huperzine A** slows acetylcholine breakdown while the choline supplies the raw material — the two are complementary.
With **uridine (UMP)** these feed the **Kennedy pathway** for neuronal membrane phospholipid synthesis — the classic uridine + DHA + choline stack.
Traditionally combined for cognition; some small trials of the pairing suggest additive effects on memory and attention, but replication is weak.

form.buy: A **standardized extract that discloses its monacolin K content per dose** and is third-party tested as **citrinin-free** (citrinin is a nephrotoxic mould by-product of the fermentation).
form.avoid: Products that do **not disclose monacolin K content** or citrinin testing — potency varies by more than 10-fold batch to batch, so an undisclosed label tells you nothing about dose.
form.withFood: Take with the **evening meal**; food increases lovastatin/monacolin K absorption and reduces GI upset.
form.bioavailability: Monacolin K is chemically **lovastatin**, a CYP3A4 substrate — grapefruit and other CYP3A4 inhibitors raise blood levels and myopathy risk.

biomarkers.why: The direct target — monacolin K inhibits HMG-CoA reductase exactly like a statin; this is the only endpoint that confirms the product is actually working.
biomarkers.range: General goal <2.6 mmol/L (lower if higher cardiovascular risk)

biomarkers.why: Shares statins' rare hepatotoxicity; a baseline plus a follow-up catches the uncommon transaminase rise.
biomarkers.range: Within lab reference; investigate if >3x upper limit

biomarkers.why: Check only if unexplained muscle pain, weakness or dark urine appears — screens for statin-type myopathy/rhabdomyolysis.
biomarkers.range: Marked elevation (e.g. >5-10x upper limit) with symptoms warrants stopping and medical review

depletes.role: HMG-CoA reductase sits upstream of both cholesterol and CoQ10 synthesis, so monacolin K lowers circulating CoQ10 — the same mechanism as prescription statins.

foodFirst.line: **Diet moves LDL through the same lever first**: ~5-10 g/day soluble fibre (oats, legumes, psyllium), 2 g/day plant sterols, and cutting saturated fat each independently lower LDL without statin-class risks.
foodFirst.note: These stack additively and carry none of the citrinin or myopathy concerns — worth exhausting before, or alongside, any monacolin product.

dosing.note: Even **3 mg monacolin K/day meaningfully lowers LDL** in trials; older efficacy studies used up to ~10 mg. Because label-to-actual content varies enormously, powder weight (~1,200-2,400 mg of rice) is meaningless — dose by disclosed monacolin K, not milligrams of rice.
dosing.cap: Keep the dose as low as is effective. EFSA found serious adverse effects (including rhabdomyolysis) reported from **3 mg/day** and set no safe intake level; the EU restricts products to <3 mg monacolins per serving. Higher intake is an unmonitored statin dose.

timing.line: Take in the **evening**: endogenous cholesterol synthesis peaks overnight and monacolin K, like lovastatin, is short-acting — evening dosing matches the enzyme's activity window.

cycling.line: **Not cycled** — LDL lowering persists only while dosing continues and rebounds within weeks of stopping; there is no benefit to intermittent use and no tolerance to reset.

contra.advice: Monacolin K IS a statin — stacking it adds unmonitored dose and multiplies myopathy/liver risk. Do not combine; treat it as mutually exclusive with prescription statin therapy.
Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal risk; the same applies to monacolin K. Avoid entirely.
Shares statins' hepatotoxic potential — avoid with active liver disease and get baseline liver enzymes first.
Grapefruit, certain antifungals, macrolides and others raise monacolin K blood levels and can precipitate rhabdomyolysis — separate or avoid.

contra.flag: **New muscle pain, weakness or dark urine**
contra.advice: Stop and seek medical review — these are the warning signs of statin-type myopathy/rhabdomyolysis.

quality.line: The two dominant quality risks are **undisclosed/variable monacolin K potency** and **citrinin contamination** (a nephrotoxic mycotoxin). Insist on a product that states monacolin K per serving and provides third-party citrinin testing.

nonResponders.line: An apparent **non-response is usually a low-potency batch**, not your biology — independent testing has found many products with near-zero monacolin K. If LDL hasn't moved at 8-12 weeks, suspect the product before concluding it doesn't work for you.

synergy.why: Replaces the CoQ10 that monacolin K depletes; evidence that it eases statin-associated muscle complaints is mixed and not endorsed by all guidelines.
Lower LDL by absorption and synthesis pathways distinct from HMG-CoA inhibition, so the effect is additive to monacolin K.

form.buy: Look for **R-ALA (R-lipoic acid)** or stabilized **sodium R-lipoate** for better absorption; note the classic diabetic-neuropathy trials used cheaper **racemic (R/S) ALA** at 600 mg, so racemic is a legitimate, evidence-backed choice.
form.avoid: **Degraded R-ALA** — the free acid polymerizes with heat/light and clumps; discoloured or fused capsules have lost potency. Sodium-salt or racemic forms are more stable.
form.withFood: Take on an **empty stomach**, ~30-60 min before eating; co-ingested food markedly lowers plasma peaks.
form.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability is only **~30%** with a very short half-life (~30 min) and heavy first-pass hepatic metabolism — hence divided or higher doses.

biomarkers.why: ALA's best-studied use beyond neuropathy is **glycemic/insulin-sensitivity support**; track over months if that's your goal.
ALA can modestly **lower blood glucose** and improve insulin sensitivity — worth monitoring, especially if on glucose-lowering meds.

depletes.role: ALA **competes with biotin (and pantothenate) for the SMVT transporter**, so chronic high-dose use may modestly lower biotin uptake; a small biotin top-up is a reasonable precaution.

foodFirst.line: Dietary ALA (spinach, broccoli, organ meats) is **tiny and protein-bound**, so almost none is free/absorbable — meaning food realistically cannot deliver therapeutic amounts.
foodFirst.note: This is one of the few antioxidants where supplementation, not diet, is the only way to reach studied doses.

cost.perDose: ~SGD 0.15-0.50 per 300-600 mg racemic dose; R-ALA / sodium R-lipoate typically 2-3x that.
cost.math: A 600 mg racemic bottle of 60-90 caps often runs ~SGD 15-30.
cost.note: Racemic ALA is inexpensive; you pay a premium mainly for the R-isomer and stabilized salt forms.

dosing.cap: **600 mg/day** is the trial-validated dose for diabetic neuropathy (ALADIN/SYDNEY trials); up to 1200 mg is used but adds GI upset without clearly more benefit.
dosing.note: Splitting into 2 doses can ease nausea; per-kg dosing is not standard for ALA.

timing.line: Take in the **morning on an empty stomach**; if nausea occurs, split into two smaller doses rather than taking with a large meal.

cycling.line: No established need to cycle; ALA is used continuously in trials. There is **no evidence of tolerance or dependence**.

contra.advice: ALA can **add to glucose lowering** — monitor for hypoglycemia and coordinate dose changes with your doctor.

ALA is a recognised trigger of **Insulin Autoimmune Syndrome (Hirata disease)**, strongly linked to HLA-DRB1*04:06 that is more common in East Asians; unexplained fasting/spontaneous hypoglycemia after starting ALA warrants stopping and medical review.
ALA may **blunt T4-to-T3 conversion** (shown when co-administered with thyroxine); monitor thyroid labs if you have thyroid dysfunction and separate dosing from thyroid meds.

contra.flag: Thiamine deficiency / heavy alcohol use or pregnancy
contra.advice: Correct thiamine first and avoid in pregnancy — **safety data are insufficient**.

quality.line: Choose **third-party-tested** product (NSF/USP-type verification); because R-ALA degrades easily, stabilized salt forms and proper packaging matter more here than for most antioxidants.

nonResponders.line: For nerve symptoms, a meaningful subset see **little benefit**; the racemic form used in trials targets diabetic neuropathy specifically, and non-diabetic 'general antioxidant' benefits are much less certain.

synergy.why: ALA's signature mechanism is **recycling other antioxidants** back to their active form, extending their effect in both water- and fat-soluble compartments.
Commonly **combined for diabetic neuropathy**, hitting complementary pathways of glucose-driven nerve damage.
Paired in **mitochondrial and neuropathy** protocols for additive support of energy metabolism and nerve function.

form.buy: Choose **PQQ disodium salt**, **L-ergothioneine**, and a **bioavailable quercetin form** (enzymatically-modified isoquercitrin, or a phospholipid/lecithin complex) — all high-purity and third-party tested.
form.avoid: Plain **quercetin dihydrate** alone is poorly absorbed, and oral **L-carnosine** is largely hydrolysed in serum, so it acts mostly as a beta-alanine/histidine source rather than delivering intact carnosine to tissue.
form.withFood: Take **quercetin with a fat-containing meal** (it is lipophilic); PQQ, ergothioneine and carnosine can be taken with or without food.
form.bioavailability: **Ergothioneine** is actively taken up via the OCTN1 transporter and accumulates in tissues with a very long half-life (roughly a week to a month), while **quercetin aglycone** has notoriously low and variable oral bioavailability.

biomarkers.why: Quercetin at roughly 500 mg/day or more modestly lowers systemic inflammation in meta-analyses (WMD around -0.3 mg/L), mainly in people with elevated baseline CRP.
biomarkers.range: <1 mg/L low, 1-3 average, >3 elevated cardiovascular risk

biomarkers.why: Quercetin inhibits xanthine oxidase and produces small reductions in serum urate, mainly in those with higher-range or pre-hyperuricaemic levels.
Quercetin at 500 mg/day or more produces small reductions in systolic/diastolic BP, more so in hypertensives.
biomarkers.when: Home cuff, baseline then at 8 weeks or later (meta-analysis effect appears mainly at doses over 500 mg/day and durations of at least 8 weeks)

antagonists.nutrient: Non-heme iron (and to a lesser degree zinc/copper)
antagonists.role: Quercetin chelates dietary non-heme iron and can blunt its absorption — separate quercetin from iron supplements or iron-rich meals by about 2 hours.

foodFirst.line: **Mushrooms** (especially oyster, king oyster and shiitake) are by far the richest **ergothioneine** source, and **onions, capers, apples and kale** top the **quercetin** list — a mushroom-and-allium-heavy diet delivers meaningful amounts of both.
foodFirst.note: **Carnosine** comes from red meat and poultry, so omnivores already get some; **PQQ** occurs in natto, parsley, green tea and fermented soy but only in microgram amounts, so diet alone won't reach studied supplement doses.

cost.math: Quercetin (500-1000 mg) is cheapest at roughly SGD 0.15-0.35/day; PQQ (20 mg) around SGD 0.50-1.00/day; ergothioneine is the priciest of the four per mg.
cost.note: Running all four daily is realistically SGD 40-90/month depending on doses and forms.

dosing.note: Typical studied ranges: **PQQ 10-20 mg**, **quercetin 500-1000 mg** (split, or lower with enzymatically-modified isoquercitrin or phospholipid-complex forms), **L-carnosine 500-1000 mg**, **ergothioneine ~5-30 mg**. No weight-based dosing is established for any of these.
dosing.cap: Chronic quercetin above ~1 g/day adds drug-interaction risk without clear extra benefit.

timing.line: Take **PQQ in the morning** (mildly activating for some); **split quercetin** across meals with fat; ergothioneine and carnosine timing is flexible since ergothioneine accumulates over weeks regardless of when it is taken.

cycling.line: None require cycling for everyday antioxidant use — daily is fine. The experimental **senolytic 'hit-and-run'** protocol (intermittent high-dose quercetin, usually paired with prescription dasatinib) is a distinct, unproven-in-healthy-people use, not the daily approach described here.

contra.advice: **Quercetin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein** and can raise levels of drugs such as ciclosporin, some statins and certain chemotherapy agents — check with a pharmacist before combining.
Quercetin can modestly lower blood pressure and has mild antiplatelet activity — watch for additive effects.
Safety data for supplemental doses of all four are insufficient — best avoided.

contra.flag: Impaired kidney function / very high quercetin doses
contra.advice: Very high (mainly intravenous) quercetin has shown dose-limiting renal-toxicity signals in trials; keep oral doses moderate if kidney function is reduced.

quality.line: Prefer products with **third-party testing** (NSF, USP or Informed Choice); for quercetin confirm the **specific form** (enzymatically-modified isoquercitrin or a phospholipid complex vs plain dihydrate), and for PQQ confirm it is standardised **disodium salt**.

nonResponders.line: 'No effect' from quercetin usually means the **plain dihydrate form wasn't absorbed** — switch to enzymatically-modified isoquercitrin or a phospholipid-complex form, or take it with fat and vitamin C. For carnosine, high **serum carnosinase** activity rapidly degrades the oral dose, so **beta-alanine** raises muscle carnosine far more reliably.

synergy.why: PQQ and CoQ10 act on complementary steps of mitochondrial energy production and have been studied together for this reason.
Vitamin C helps regenerate and stabilise oxidised quercetin, improving its practical antioxidant effect.
Commonly co-formulated with quercetin, adding its own proteolytic anti-inflammatory activity (the claimed absorption boost is not well substantiated).
Another flavonoid senolytic often stacked with quercetin in longevity protocols, though the senolytic evidence is still largely preclinical.

form.buy: **PHGG (partially hydrolysed guar gum)** is the best-tolerated and most versatile — flavourless, dissolves clear, minimal gas. **GOS** and **inulin/FOS** are effective but more gas-forming. For butyrate directly, **tributyrin** or enteric-coated/**calcium-magnesium butyrate** delivers it lower in the gut than plain sodium butyrate.
form.avoid: Cheap inulin sold as a generic 'fibre supplement' at high starting doses; agave-derived inulin syrups (mostly sugar). Non-enteric sodium butyrate is largely absorbed in the upper gut and smells strongly of rancid butter.
form.withFood: Prebiotic powders mix into water, yoghurt or coffee and can be taken with or without food; butyrate capsules are usually taken with meals.
form.bioavailability: Prebiotics are not 'absorbed' — they reach the colon intact and are fermented by resident bacteria into short-chain fatty acids including butyrate, which is why effects build over 2-4 weeks as the microbiome adapts.

foodFirst.line: **Food first is the default here.** Whole-food fibres feed the microbiome more diversely than any single supplement: onions, garlic, leeks and chicory (inulin/FOS), slightly-green bananas and cooked-then-cooled potato/rice/oats (resistant starch), legumes (GOS), plus oats and barley (beta-glucan).
foodFirst.note: Diversity of plant fibres matters more than total grams — aim for many different plants weekly. Supplements are a tool to top up a hard-to-hit fibre target (~25-38 g/day), not a replacement for eating plants.

cost.math: A 500 g tub of PHGG or inulin runs ~S$25-40; at a 5-7 g dose that is roughly 70-100 servings.
cost.note: Prebiotic fibre is one of the cheapest evidence-backed gut interventions; butyrate capsules cost more (~S$0.40-1.00/dose) for less clear added benefit if fibre intake is already adequate.

dosing.note: Start low and titrate: PHGG ~2-3 g/day up toward 5-10 g; inulin/FOS ~2-3 g up toward 5-10 g; GOS ~1-2 g up toward 3-5 g. Increase every 3-7 days as tolerated. Going straight to a full dose is the most common cause of the bloating and gas that makes people quit.
dosing.cap: Most trials use ~5-15 g/day of a given prebiotic; gas tends to rise with little added benefit beyond that.

timing.line: Timing is not critical — consistency matters far more than clock time. Splitting into two smaller doses (e.g. morning and evening) reduces gas versus one large dose.

cycling.line: No need to cycle — prebiotics work by sustaining a fibre-fed microbiome, so benefits depend on continued daily intake and fade within days to weeks of stopping.

contra.flag: IBS, SIBO, or a low-FODMAP diet
contra.advice: Inulin/FOS and GOS are high-FODMAP and can worsen bloating, pain and diarrhoea. **PHGG is low-FODMAP and usually the tolerated choice** — start very low and consider working with a dietitian.

contra.flag: Transient gas and bloating on starting
contra.advice: Expected and dose-dependent as bacteria adapt; drop back to the last comfortable dose and re-titrate more slowly. Persistent severe symptoms mean stop and reassess.

contra.flag: Active flare of IBD or significant GI disease
contra.advice: Do not self-experiment during a flare; fibre tolerance varies and should be guided by your gastroenterologist.

quality.line: Choose a **single-ingredient powder with the specific fibre and gram amount stated** (not a proprietary 'fibre blend'). Independent third-party testing (e.g. NSF, Informed Sport) is a neutral marker of label accuracy and contaminant screening.

nonResponders.line: Response varies with your starting microbiome — some people already eating a high-fibre diet notice little, while low-fibre eaters gain the most. A minority tolerate fermentable prebiotics poorly no matter how slowly they titrate; PHGG or plain dietary fibre is the fallback.

synergy.why: Prebiotic fibre provides the fermentable substrate that live bacteria need to establish and produce short-chain fatty acids.
A different fermentable substrate that is particularly butyrogenic, broadening the fibres your microbiome can convert to butyrate.

synergy.with: Dietary polyphenols (berries, cocoa, green tea, coffee)
synergy.why: Polyphenols and fibre together may shift the microbiome toward beneficial fibre-fermenting species more than either alone.
Bulking and viscous fibres like PHGG work better and cause less discomfort when fluid intake is sufficient.

form.buy: **Zinc-carnosine**: the chelated 1:1 zinc-L-carnosine complex (polaprezinc), typically 75 mg tablets. **S. boulardii**: lyophilized, labeled by guaranteed CFU (roughly 5-10 billion). **Akkermansia muciniphila**: the pasteurized form carries the only human RCT data; choose products with cell count guaranteed to expiry. **DGL**: deglycyrrhizinated licorice (glycyrrhizin removed). Prefer high-purity, third-party-tested products generically over any brand.
form.avoid: Regular whole-licorice extract in place of DGL — its glycyrrhizin raises blood pressure and lowers potassium. Probiotic products with no guaranteed CFU count, or only a 'time of manufacture' number.
form.withFood: Chew DGL 15-20 min before meals; probiotics are fine with or just after food; zinc-carnosine can be taken with a little food to blunt nausea.
form.bioavailability: Zinc-carnosine works largely by adhering to inflamed mucosa rather than by systemic zinc absorption, so the intact chelated complex matters more than elemental zinc content.

dosing.note: Studied ranges: zinc-carnosine 75 mg once or twice daily (~17 mg elemental zinc per 75 mg; the common research regimen of 75 mg twice daily delivers ~34 mg elemental zinc/day, which exceeds typical zinc upper limits); S. boulardii 250-500 mg (~5-10 billion CFU) 1-2x daily; pasteurized Akkermansia ~10^10 cells/day; DGL ~380-400 mg chewed before meals. No per-kg dosing — these are fixed adult doses.

timing.line: **DGL is chewed before meals** so it mixes with saliva and coats the esophagus/stomach ahead of food. **S. boulardii around antibiotics** can be taken alongside them (it is a yeast, so antibacterials don't kill it) but is often spaced a couple of hours from the dose anyway. Zinc-carnosine is often split morning/evening on a relatively empty stomach for mucosal contact.

cycling.line: These are **repair/course tools, not permanent supplements**. Zinc-carnosine is typically run in ~8-week courses; S. boulardii during and for 1-2 weeks after an antibiotic course; DGL for reflux flares rather than indefinitely. Reassess and stop when the target symptom resolves.

contra.flag: Immunocompromised, critically ill, or central venous catheter
contra.advice: Live probiotic yeast (S. boulardii) has caused **fungemia** in these groups. Avoid live probiotics and discuss with a clinician first.

Sustained zinc intake depletes copper. A single 75 mg dose (~17 mg elemental zinc) is modest, but the studied twice-daily regimen (~34 mg/day) exceeds common zinc upper limits — watch copper status if run for months or stacked with other zinc.

contra.flag: Hypertension, low potassium, or on diuretics
contra.advice: Use **DGL specifically** — whole-licorice glycyrrhizin causes pseudoaldosteronism (high BP, low potassium). DGL has this removed.
Human safety data for Akkermansia supplementation is limited and short-term; avoid in pregnancy and immunocompromise until more is known.
Rule out H. pylori and red-flag causes with a clinician before self-treating long-term with DGL or zinc-carnosine — these relieve symptoms rather than diagnose.

depletes.role: Zinc competes with copper for absorption; chronic zinc intake can cause copper deficiency (anemia, neuropathy).

needs.role: Probiotic and Akkermansia benefits depend on a substrate-rich gut; fermentable fiber and polyphenols support colonization and mucus turnover.

antagonists.role: Will kill live S. boulardii, negating it — separate use or choose a bacterial probiotic instead.

biomarkers.marker: H. pylori (stool antigen or urea breath test)
biomarkers.why: Should be checked before chronically self-treating reflux/gastritis; zinc-carnosine is an adjunct to eradication, not a replacement.
biomarkers.when: Before starting a long gut-repair course

biomarkers.why: Detects copper depletion if zinc is run for months or stacked with other zinc sources.
The relevant measurable target for Akkermansia's metabolic use — track insulin sensitivity rather than gut symptoms.

foodFirst.line: **Polyphenol-rich foods raise Akkermansia naturally** — cranberry, pomegranate, grapes, red berries, green tea — as does fermentable fiber and fermented foods. Dietary zinc (oysters, red meat, shellfish) covers zinc needs; a food-diverse gut is the substrate every one of these products depends on.
foodFirst.note: For probiotics generally, feeding existing microbes with varied plants often beats adding a single strain.

cost.note: Rough SGD/day: DGL ~$0.30-0.60, zinc-carnosine ~$0.50-1, S. boulardii ~$0.50-1, Akkermansia the outlier at ~$2-4/day due to anaerobe manufacturing.

quality.line: For probiotics, demand **CFU/cell count guaranteed through shelf-life at expiry**, not at manufacture, plus third-party testing. Akkermansia is a strict anaerobe and hard to keep viable, so stability and cold-chain claims matter most there. NSF or USP verification is a neutral quality signal.

nonResponders.line: **DGL fails when reflux isn't acid-driven** — non-acid reflux, motility problems, or untreated H. pylori won't respond. Probiotic benefit is strain- and context-specific: S. boulardii's clearest win is preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea, not general 'gut health,' and it may do little without that trigger.

synergy.why: S. boulardii co-taken with antibiotics reduces antibiotic-associated and C. difficile diarrhea — its best-evidenced use.
Zinc-carnosine as an adjunct to standard triple therapy has improved eradication and mucosal healing in trials.
Feed Akkermansia and other beneficial microbes rather than dosing a probiotic into an empty substrate.
Zinc-carnosine has been studied for protecting gastric mucosa against NSAID- and stress-induced damage.

form.buy: **Acetyl-L-carnitine (ALCAR)** as the L-isomer only — the acetylated ester is the form that crosses the blood-brain barrier. Prefer high-purity, third-party-tested powder or capsules.
form.avoid: Any **D- or DL-carnitine (racemic)** form — the D-isomer is biologically inactive and can competitively interfere with L-carnitine. Plain L-carnitine tartrate is fine for muscle but is a poorer choice for CNS/cognitive goals than the acetylated form.
form.withFood: Take on a **relatively empty stomach** (water, no large meal) for better uptake; if it causes nausea, take with a light snack.
form.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability is **low and saturable** (plain L-carnitine is only ~5-25% absorbed; ALCAR is partly absorbed intact), which is why total daily intake is split into 2-3 doses rather than one large one.

dosing.cap: 3 g/day
dosing.note: Typical studied range is **1,000-2,000 mg/day**, split into 2 doses (e.g. morning + early afternoon). Trials in age-related cognitive decline and neuropathy used up to 2,000-3,000 mg/day. Start at 500 mg to gauge GI tolerance and stimulation before titrating up.

timing.line: Dose **morning and early afternoon** — ALCAR can be mildly stimulating/alerting, so late-day dosing may disrupt sleep in sensitive people.

cycling.line: **No mandatory cycling** — it is a naturally occurring metabolite. Long, continuous use is common in the literature; take periodic breaks mainly to re-check whether it is still doing anything for you.

biomarkers.why: Confirms baseline **carnitine status** — the people who respond most are those who start deficient (see non-responders).
biomarkers.when: Baseline if deficiency is suspected (vegan diet, dialysis, valproate use)

biomarkers.why: Gut bacteria convert carnitine to TMA -> TMAO, a marker **associated with cardiovascular risk**; worth watching with chronic high-dose use, especially with an already meat-heavy diet.
biomarkers.range: No firm cutoff; lower is generally viewed as better
biomarkers.when: Only if using high doses long-term and cardiovascular risk is a concern

biomarkers.why: Carnitine can **blunt thyroid hormone action** peripherally (it has been used therapeutically in hyperthyroidism); track if you are hypothyroid or on thyroid replacement.
biomarkers.range: TSH ~0.4-4.0 mIU/L; free T4 per lab range
biomarkers.when: Baseline and follow-up if thyroid status is relevant

foodFirst.line: Dietary **carnitine comes mainly from red meat and other animal products**, so most omnivores are already carnitine-replete — the supplement's edge is the acetylated, brain-penetrating form and topping up genuinely low-carnitine groups.
foodFirst.note: Food does not meaningfully supply the *acetyl* form, so the CNS-directed use case cannot be replicated by diet alone.

cost.math: A generic third-party-tested ALCAR bottle runs roughly S$25-40 for ~60-90 g; at 1-2 g/day that is about S$0.30-0.80 daily.
cost.note: One of the cheaper nootropic-category compounds; the acetyl form costs modestly more than plain L-carnitine.

quality.line: Choose products explicitly labelled **L-carnitine / acetyl-L-carnitine** (not DL-) with third-party testing (USP or NSF verification is a reasonable neutral signal); carnitine powder is hygroscopic, so avoid clumped or damp product.

nonResponders.line: People who are already **carnitine-replete** (regular meat-eaters, younger and healthy) often notice little; the clearest responders are the **carnitine-depleted** — vegetarians/vegans, older adults, and those on valproate or dialysis.

synergy.why: The classic **mitochondrial pairing** — co-supplemented in aging/energy-metabolism research (ALCAR feeds fatty-acid oxidation, ALA supports the same pathway as an antioxidant cofactor).
ALCAR donates acetyl groups toward **acetylcholine synthesis**; adequate choline provides the other half of that reaction.
Combined with ALCAR in some **cognition and mood** studies; complementary support for neuronal membranes and mitochondrial function.

contra.advice: Carnitine can **blunt thyroid hormone action** in tissues; if hypothyroid, use cautiously and monitor TSH/free T4 rather than starting high-dose blind.
L-carnitine has been reported (case-level) to **raise INR** on warfarin/coumarin anticoagulants; check with the prescriber and monitor INR before combining.
Its mild stimulating quality can worsen **agitation, restlessness, or insomnia**; start low and stop if activation appears.
Chronic high doses can cause a **trimethylaminuria-like fishy odor** via TMA production; lower the dose if it appears.

form.buy: **Soy- or sunflower-lecithin-derived PS**, ideally third-party tested; sunflower-sourced avoids soy allergens.
form.avoid: **Bovine (cow-brain) cortex PS** — the source with the best old trial data, but carries a theoretical prion/BSE transmission concern and is essentially off the market.
form.withFood: Take **with a fat-containing meal** — PS is a phospholipid and absorption is better alongside dietary fat.
form.bioavailability: Well absorbed orally; note that today's plant-derived PS has a different fatty-acid profile than the bovine PS used in the strongest cognition studies.

dosing.cap: Cognition trials mostly used ~300 mg/day; doses up to ~800 mg/day have been used in cortisol studies and appear well tolerated.
dosing.note: Common cognition regimen is **100 mg three times daily (300 mg/day)** with meals. Cortisol-blunting exercise studies used higher doses (~600-800 mg/day); some mental-stress studies used 300-400 mg/day. No per-kg dosing is established.

timing.line: Split with meals through the day for cognition; for a **stress/cortisol** aim, some dose before a known stressor such as a hard training session.

cycling.line: No established need to cycle; benefits (if any) build over **weeks of continuous use**, so trials ran daily for 6-12 weeks.

foodFirst.line: PS occurs in **organ meats, fish (mackerel, herring), and soy/sunflower lecithin**, but a typical diet supplies only ~100-150 mg/day.
foodFirst.note: You cannot realistically reach the 300 mg studied doses from food, which is the main rationale for a supplement.

cost.math: A 60-cap bottle of 100 mg runs roughly SGD 30-60; at 300 mg/day that is ~SGD 1.50-4.50/day.
cost.note: Among the pricier nootropic ingredients per gram; sunflower-sourced tends to cost more than soy.

biomarkers.why: The main mechanistic claim is blunting of the cortisol response to stress; tracking around a stressor (e.g. hard exercise) is the only objective readout.
biomarkers.range: Morning serum cortisol reference is roughly ~140-690 nmol/L (highly lab-dependent); interpret trends, not single values.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and after 4-6 weeks, ideally sampled at a consistent time of day.

contra.advice: Phospholipid supplements may theoretically add to bleeding risk; clear it with your doctor first.
Choose a clearly labeled sunflower-derived PS instead of soy-derived.
Insufficient safety data — avoid.

quality.line: Look for a product that **names its source (sunflower or soy) and PS content per serving**, and ideally carries independent third-party testing (e.g. NSF or USP); avoid any bovine-brain sourced material.

nonResponders.line: Much of the flattering memory data came from **bovine-brain PS**; plant-derived PS has weaker and mixed results, so a subjective non-response is common and expected.

synergy.why: PS is a membrane phospholipid, much of it DHA-enriched; the two are often paired, and adequate DHA supports the membranes PS is meant to build.

form.buy: **Enteric-coated tablets** in foil blister packs — SAM-e is degraded by stomach acid, heat and moisture, so coating and sealed packaging protect the active dose.
form.avoid: Non-enteric-coated tablets, loose bottles, or product left in a warm humid room; the molecule oxidizes readily and loses potency.
form.withFood: Take on an **empty stomach**, ~20-30 min before a meal, to improve absorption.
form.bioavailability: Sold as stabilized salts — **1,4-butanedisulfonate (disulfonate)** or **tosylate**; the disulfonate form is generally the more stable. Label should state the salt and the yield of active ion.

biomarkers.why: SAM-e is converted to SAH then homocysteine; without adequate B12/folate/B6 to recycle it, blood homocysteine can rise.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and after ~8 weeks, especially if B-vitamin status is uncertain

needs.role: Remethylate homocysteine back to methionine, keeping the methylation cycle running and limiting homocysteine build-up.
Cofactor for the transsulfuration pathway that clears homocysteine downstream of SAM-e.

depletes.role: Heavy methyl-donor turnover can unmask a marginal folate/B12 deficiency by raising homocysteine demand.

foodFirst.line: SAM-e is **made in the body** from the amino acid methionine plus ATP — it is not obtained in meaningful amounts from food.
foodFirst.note: Adequate protein (methionine) plus folate and B12 support your own SAM-e production; the supplement is a direct top-up, not a dietary replacement.

cost.math: A typical 800-1600 mg/day dose runs ~S$1.50-6/day — one of the pricier single supplements.
cost.note: Rough estimate; enteric-coated products sit at the higher end. Confirm current Singapore pricing.

dosing.cap: ~1600 mg/day
dosing.note: Mood: commonly 800-1600 mg/day (studies span 400-1600). Osteoarthritis: ~600-1200 mg/day. Start low (200-400 mg) and titrate up over days to reduce GI upset, jitteriness or anxiety. Split into two doses.

timing.line: Take in the **morning (and midday)**, not late — SAM-e can be activating and evening doses may disrupt sleep.

cycling.line: No cycling needed; but allow **2-6 weeks** for a mood effect and up to ~4 weeks for osteoarthritis before judging it.

contra.advice: SAM-e can trigger mania or hypomania and switch mood states — avoid unless supervised by a psychiatrist.
Additive serotonin effect raises serotonin-syndrome risk (case reports); only combine under medical supervision.
SAM-e can methylate and may reduce the effect of levodopa — discuss with your neurologist first.
Data are limited outside specialist obstetric use (e.g. cholestasis); do not self-prescribe.

quality.line: Choose **enteric-coated, blister-sealed** products that disclose the salt form and active-ion yield; independent testing (e.g. USP/NSF) helps, as cheaper SAM-e often assays below its label claim.

nonResponders.line: If no benefit after ~6 weeks at an adequate dose — or if B12/folate are low — it may not be your lever; correct B-vitamin status first and reassess.

synergy.why: Keeps homocysteine in check and supports the methylation cycle SAM-e feeds into.
An alternative methyl donor that helps remethylate homocysteine, complementing SAM-e's methyl turnover.

form.buy: High-purity extract **standardized to 70-80% silymarin** (the flavonolignan complex), ideally listing **silybin** content. A **silybin-phosphatidylcholine (phytosome) complex** absorbs better than plain extract.
form.avoid: Whole-herb powder or products with no standardization percentage on the label - potency is unpredictable.
form.withFood: Take **with a fat-containing meal** - the flavonolignans are poorly water-soluble and dietary fat improves the modest absorption available.
form.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability is **inherently low** (silybin is poorly water-soluble and rapidly conjugated/excreted). Phytosome and other complexed forms raise plasma silybin roughly **2-5x** over plain extract.

biomarkers.why: The most-used endpoint in silymarin liver trials; track for any personal change, but note **large human reviews show no consistent benefit**.
Paired with ALT to gauge hepatocellular status; interpret alongside ALT rather than alone.
biomarkers.when: With ALT at baseline and ~8-12 weeks

biomarkers.why: Sensitive marker of hepatobiliary stress and alcohol load; useful personal baseline if liver is the goal.

antagonists.role: Silybin **inhibits CYP2C9** at clinically achievable concentrations; effect in vivo appears modest but is relevant for narrow-margin drugs metabolized by it (e.g. warfarin).

cost.perDose: ~S$0.10-0.40 per 140 mg dose of standardized extract
cost.math: A bottle of standardized extract runs roughly S$15-30 for 60-120 doses; phytosome forms cost noticeably more.
cost.note: One of the cheaper botanicals; phytosome/complexed forms carry a real price premium for the bioavailability gain.

dosing.cap: Trials commonly use ~420 mg/day (some NAFLD trials up to ~600 mg/day)
dosing.note: Typical research dosing is **~140 mg silymarin, 2-3x daily**, taken with meals. There is no established per-kg dosing. Complexed/phytosome forms use lower silybin doses because absorption is higher.

timing.line: Split across meals (e.g. **with breakfast and dinner**); pairing with dietary fat aids the limited absorption. No compelling reason for a specific time of day.

contra.advice: Milk thistle is in the daisy family; those allergic to ragweed, chrysanthemum, marigold or daisy can react - **avoid or trial cautiously**.
Possible **CYP2C9 interaction**; if on warfarin or similar, discuss with a clinician and monitor rather than self-adding.
Safety data are insufficient; **avoid** unless directed by a clinician.
Silymarin shows **weak, mixed estrogenic activity** in lab models; if you have an estrogen-sensitive condition, be cautious and discuss with a clinician.

quality.line: Choose a product **standardized to a stated silymarin percentage** and ideally **third-party tested** (e.g. USP or NSF verified) - botanical extracts vary widely in actual flavonolignan content.

nonResponders.line: Because plain-extract absorption is so low, many people see **no change in liver enzymes** - unsurprising given null findings in large human reviews. For a fairer trial, a **phytosome/complexed form** delivers meaningfully more silybin.

synergy.why: Complexing silybin with phosphatidylcholine markedly **improves absorption** of an otherwise poorly bioavailable molecule.
Both support **glutathione/antioxidant pathways** in the liver via different mechanisms; a plausible complementary pairing, though outcome data are limited.

form.buy: **Standardized root extract** (capsule or tablet) listing valerenic acid content (commonly ~0.8%); hydroalcoholic or aqueous extracts are the best-studied forms.
form.avoid: **Undosed 'sleep blend' products** where valerian is one of many herbs at unlisted amounts, and cheap root powders with no standardization.
form.withFood: Take **with or without food**; capsules bypass the strong isovaleric-acid smell that makes the tea unpleasant.
form.bioavailability: Active constituents (**valerenic acid, valepotriates**) are not fully characterized, so 'standardization' is an imperfect proxy for potency.

foodFirst.line: Valerian is **not a dietary nutrient** — there is no food source, and it is not correcting a deficiency.
foodFirst.note: Fix the free levers first: **consistent sleep-wake timing, caffeine cutoff ~8h before bed, and a dark cool room** outperform any sleep herb and cost nothing.

cost.math: A bottle of 60–90 standardized capsules runs ~$15–35 SGD; at 1–2 caps/night that is roughly $0.20–0.60 per dose.
cost.note: Cheap relative to prescription hypnotics, but the modest and inconsistent benefit means it is easy to overpay for a placebo-grade effect.

dosing.cap: ~900 mg extract/night
dosing.note: Typical trials use **300–600 mg of standardized extract** 30–120 min before bed (or ~2–3 g dried root as tea). No per-kg dosing exists. Benefit in some studies only appears after **1–2 weeks of nightly use**, not the first dose.

timing.line: Take **30 minutes to 2 hours before bed**; for chronic use, dose nightly for 1–2 weeks before judging effect rather than relying on it acutely.

cycling.line: Not a controlled sedative and no clear physical dependence, but long-term safety is poorly studied — **use as-needed or in short courses** and avoid indefinite nightly reliance. Some report grogginess or rebound restlessness on abrupt stop after prolonged use.

contra.flag: Alcohol, benzodiazepines, or other CNS depressants
contra.advice: **Additive sedation** — do not stack; combining can cause excess drowsiness and impaired coordination.
**Insufficient safety data** — avoid unless cleared by a clinician.
Stop **at least 1–2 weeks before surgery** (tapering gradually after long-term use) due to additive effects with anesthetics/sedatives.
May cause **next-morning drowsiness or impaired alertness** — do not drive until you know how it affects you.
Rare **hepatotoxicity reports** (mostly multi-herb products) — use caution and stop if jaundice, dark urine, or right-upper-quadrant pain appears.

quality.line: Choose products **standardized to valerenic acid and third-party tested** (e.g. USP or NSF certified); herbal supplements are loosely regulated and content varies widely between brands.

nonResponders.line: A **large fraction of people get no meaningful benefit** — trial results are genuinely mixed. If sleep hasn't improved after 2–4 weeks of consistent nightly use, stop rather than escalating the dose.

synergy.why: The **valerian–hops combination** is the best-studied pairing and shows more consistent mild sleep benefit than valerian alone in several RCTs.
Traditional GABAergic/anxiolytic pairing; some small trials suggest additive calming effect on sleep onset.
